title.zh: 卷六十六 陳王列傳
title.raw: 卷六十六 陳王列傳 Volume 66: Biographies of Chen, Wang

sentences.zh: 陳蕃字仲舉，汝南平輿人也。

translations.literal: Chen Fan, courtesy name Zhongju, was a native of Pingyu in Runan commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Fan, whose courtesy name was Zhongju, came from Pingyu in Runan.

sentences.zh: 祖河東太守。

translations.literal: His grandfather had served as Administrator of Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather had been governor of Hedong.

sentences.zh: 蕃年十五，嘗閒處一室，而庭宇蕪穢。

translations.literal: When Fan was fifteen, he once stayed alone in a room while the courtyard was overgrown and neglected.
translations.idiomatic: At fifteen, Fan once sat idle in a single room while weeds choked the courtyard.

sentences.zh: 父友同郡薛勤來候之，謂蕃曰：「孺子何不灑埽以待賓客？

translations.literal: His father's friend Xue Qin, from the same commandery, came to visit and said to Fan, "Young sir, why do you not sprinkle water and sweep to receive guests?"
translations.idiomatic: A friend of his father’s, Xue Qin from the same commandery, paid a call and asked, “Why not tidy the place if you mean to entertain visitors?”

sentences.zh: 」蕃曰：「大丈夫處世，當埽除天下，安事一室乎！

translations.literal: Fan said, "A great man in this world ought to sweep away evil from the realm—why should he fuss over one room!"
translations.idiomatic: Fan replied, “A man of stature clears injustice from the realm—what are four walls to him?”

sentences.zh: 」勤知其有清世志，甚奇之。

translations.literal: Qin understood that he had the ambition to purify the age and marvelled greatly at him.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing that Fan meant to set the world to rights, Qin was deeply impressed.

sentences.zh: 初仕郡，舉孝廉，除郎中。

translations.literal: He first served in the commandery administration, was nominated Filial and Incorrupt, and was appointed to the post of Gentlemen of the Palace.
translations.idiomatic: He began his career in the local administration, earned nomination as Filial and Incorrupt, and received appointment as a Gentlemen of the Palace.

sentences.zh: 遭母憂，□官行喪。

translations.literal: He encountered mourning for his mother, left office, and observed the rites.
translations.idiomatic: When his mother died, he resigned his post and observed mourning.

sentences.zh: 服闋，刺史周景辟別駕從事，[一] 以諫爭不合，投傳而去。

translations.literal: When the mourning period ended, the Inspector Zhou Jing appointed him Attendant Clerk for the Separate Carriage; [1] because they could not agree in remonstrance, he cast aside his tallies and departed.
translations.idiomatic: After the mourning term ended, Inspector Zhou Jing brought him on as Attendant Clerk for the Separate Carriage. [1] They argued repeatedly until Fan threw down his credentials and walked away.

sentences.zh: [二]後公府辟舉方正，皆不就。

translations.literal: [2] Later the high offices summoned him on recommendation as Incorrupt and Upright; he did not take any of the posts.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Later the central government repeatedly recommended him as Upright and Incorrupt; he declined every summons.

sentences.zh: 注[一]續漢志曰：「別駕從事，校尉行部奉引，總錄觿事。」

translations.literal: Note [1] The Continued Treatise on Han says: "The Attendant Clerk for the Separate Carriage, when the regional inspector tours the commandery, leads the escort and has general oversight of the tour's business."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] The Continued Treatise on Han explains that the Attendant Clerk for the Separate Carriage leads the escort when the regional inspector makes his rounds and coordinates all official business on that circuit.

sentences.zh: 注[二]投，□也。

translations.literal: Note [2] "Cast aside" means to throw away.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] The verb rendered “cast aside” means to discard.

sentences.zh: 傳謂符也，音丁戀反。

translations.literal: "Tallies" means the official tallies; the pronunciation is ding-luan fan.
translations.idiomatic: “Tallies” denotes the official credentials; it is read with the fan pronunciation associated with “ding-luan.”

sentences.zh: 太尉李固表薦，征拜議郎，再遷為樂安太守。

translations.literal: The Grand Commandant Li Gu presented a memorial recommending him; he was summoned and appointed Court Gentleman, then transferred twice to become Administrator of Le'an.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Commandant Li Gu recommended him in a memorial; the court summoned Fan as a Court Gentleman and soon promoted him twice, making him governor of Le’an.

sentences.zh: [一]時李膺為青州刺史，名有威政，屬城聞風，皆自引去，蕃獨以清績留。

translations.literal: [1] At that time Li Ying was Regional Governor of Qing Province, famed for stern government; the subordinate commanderies, hearing of his reputation, saw their officials withdraw on their own, but Fan alone stayed on account of his clean record.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Li Ying was then governor of Qing Province, where his reputation for tough governance persuaded many county magistrates to resign—yet Fan remained, widely noted for an honest administration.

sentences.zh: 郡人周璆，高絜之士。

translations.literal: Zhou Qiu of the commandery was a man of lofty integrity.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Qiu, a man of the commandery, was celebrated for moral purity.

sentences.zh: [二]前後郡守招命莫肯至，唯蕃能致焉。

translations.literal: [2] Former and later administrators had summoned him with orders, but he would not come; only Fan could bring him in.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Successive governors had invited him without success; Fan alone won him over.

sentences.zh: 字而不名，特為置一榻，去則縣之。

translations.literal: [Fan] addressed him by his courtesy name and not his personal name, and had a special couch set out for him; when [Zhou] left, [Fan] hung the couch up.
translations.idiomatic: Fan called him by his style, not his given name, reserved a couch just for him, and stored it away whenever Zhou departed—an exceptional mark of respect.

sentences.zh: 璆字孟玉，臨濟人，有美名。

translations.literal: Qiu's courtesy name was Mengyu; he was a man of Linji and enjoyed a fine reputation.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Qiu, courtesy Mengyu, hailed from Linji and carried an esteemed name.

sentences.zh: 民有趙宣葬親而不閉埏隧，[三]因居其中，行服二十餘年，鄉邑稱孝，州郡數禮請之。

translations.literal: The commoner Zhao Xuan had buried his parents but had not closed the passage into the tomb; [3] he dwelt inside it, observing mourning for more than twenty years, and the township praised his filial piety; the province and commandery several times respectfully invited him.
translations.idiomatic: [3] A commoner named Zhao Xuan left the tomb passage open after burying his parents, moved inside, and kept mourning for over twenty years; neighbors hailed him as a paragon of filial piety, and provincial authorities repeatedly tried to honor him.

sentences.zh: 郡內以薦蕃，蕃與相見，問及妻子，而宣五子皆服中所生。

translations.literal: The commandery staff therefore recommended him to Fan. When Fan met him and asked about his wife and children, it turned out that Xuan's five sons had all been born during the mourning period.
translations.idiomatic: Local officers touted Zhao to Fan. At their meeting Fan asked after Zhao’s family and learned that all five sons had been conceived while Zhao pretended to mourn inside the tomb.

sentences.zh: 蕃大怒曰：「聖人制禮，賢者俯就，不肖企及。

translations.literal: Fan was greatly angered and said, "The sages established the rites; the worthy bend themselves to follow them, while the unworthy strain upward to reach them.
translations.idiomatic: Fan’s temper flared: “The sages framed ritual so that the worthy can humble themselves to it and the unworthy may still stretch toward it.”

sentences.zh: [四]且祭不欲數，以其易黷故也。

translations.literal: [4] Moreover sacrificial offerings should not be frequent, because frequency leads to disrespect."
translations.idiomatic: [4] Sacrifices must not be multiplied; repetition breeds irreverence.”

sentences.zh: [五]況乃寢宿頤藏，而孕育其中，誑時惑觿，誣污鬼神乎？

translations.literal: [5] How much worse to sleep and breed inside a burial vault, deceiving the age and deluding the common people, and insulting the spirits!"
translations.idiomatic: [5] What of hiding in a grave to beget children—duping the public and defiling the dead?”

sentences.zh: 」遂致其罪。

translations.literal: He thereupon had him charged with an offense.
translations.idiomatic: He pressed charges against Zhao Xuan.

sentences.zh: 注[一]續漢志曰，樂安本名千乘，和帝更名也。

translations.literal: Note [1] The Continued Treatise on Han says: Le'an was originally called Qiansheng; Emperor He changed the name.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] The Continued Treatise on Han records that Le’an was once called Qiansheng until Emperor He renamed it.

sentences.zh: 注[二]璆音仇。

translations.literal: Note [2] The character 璆 is read like 仇 (qiu).
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] The graph 璆 is pronounced like 仇.

sentences.zh: 注[三]埏隧，今人墓道也。

translations.literal: Note [3] *Yan sui* is what people today call the tomb passage.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3] *Yan sui* refers to the underground passage of a tomb.

sentences.zh: 杜預注左傳云：「掘地通路曰隧。」

translations.literal: Du Yu's commentary on the *Zuo Tradition* says, "Cutting a passage through the earth is called a *sui*."
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu’s commentary on the *Zuo Tradition* defines a tunnel cut through earth as a *sui*.

sentences.zh: 注[四]禮記曰「三年之喪，可復父母之恩也。

translations.literal: Note [4] The *Record of Rites* says, "The mourning of three years can repay parents' kindness.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4] The *Record of Rites* states that three years of mourning repay parental kindness.

sentences.zh: 賢者俯而就之，不肖者企而及之。」

translations.literal: The worthy bend down to it; the unworthy stretch up to reach it."
translations.idiomatic: The worthy bow themselves into it; the unworthy still strive to meet it.”

sentences.zh: 注[五]黷，媟也。

translations.literal: Note [5] *Du* means frivolous, disrespectful.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5] *Du* means irreverent excess.

sentences.zh: 禮記曰：「祭不欲數，數則煩，煩則不敬。」

translations.literal: The *Record of Rites* says: "Sacrifices should not be frequent; if frequent, they become tiresome, and if tiresome they are not reverent."
translations.idiomatic: The same classic adds that offerings must not be repeated too often, lest they become routine and lose awe.

sentences.zh: 大將軍梁冀威震天下，時遣書詣蕃，有所請托，不得通，使者詐求謁，蕃怒，笞殺之，坐左轉修武令。

translations.literal: The great general Liang Ji inspired awe throughout the realm; he sent a letter to Fan with a private request, but it could not get through. The messenger feigned a request for an audience; Fan, enraged, had him beaten to death with the bamboo, and for that was demoted by transfer to the left to be magistrate of Xiuwu.
translations.idiomatic: General-in-chief Liang Ji dominated the court and sent Fan a note seeking a favor, but Fan refused the channel. When a courier lied his way into an audience, Fan had him flogged to death and was consequently demoted to magistrate of Xiuwu.

sentences.zh: 稍遷，拜尚書。

translations.literal: He was gradually promoted and appointed Master of Writing.
translations.idiomatic: He rose again and became a Master of Writing.

sentences.zh: 時零陵、桂陽山賊為害，公卿議遣討之，又詔下州郡，一切皆得舉孝廉、茂才。

translations.literal: At that time bandits in the mountains of Lingling and Guilin were doing harm. The high officials discussed sending troops to attack them, and an edict also went down to the provinces and commanderies that in all cases one might nominate candidates for Filial and Incorrupt and for Abundant Talent.
translations.idiomatic: While Lingling and Guilin bandits terrorized the hills, ministers debated a punitive expedition, and an edict invited every province to nominate Filial and Incorrupt men and men of abundant talent without restriction.

sentences.zh: 蕃上疏駁之曰：「昔高祖創業，萬邦息肩，撫養百姓，同之赤子。

translations.literal: Fan presented a memorial refuting this, saying: "In olden times the Founding Emperor established the enterprise; the myriad regions laid down their burdens; he nurtured the common people and treated them like infants in arms.
translations.idiomatic: Fan answered with a memorial: “When Gaozu founded the dynasty, he let the realm catch its breath and cared for the people as his own babes.

sentences.zh: [一]今二郡之民，亦陛下赤子也。

translations.literal: [1] The people of these two commanderies are also Your Majesty's infants in arms.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The folk of those two commanderies are no less your babes.”

sentences.zh: 致令赤子為害，豈非所在貪虐，使其然乎？

translations.literal: To have brought it about that infants in arms become a scourge—is it not because those in power were greedy and cruel and made them so?
translations.idiomatic: If those babes have turned into rebels, is it not because local officials bled them dry?

sentences.zh: 宜嚴□三府，隱核牧守令長，其有在政失和，侵暴百姓者，即便舉奏，更選清賢奉公之人，能班宣法令情在愛惠者，可不勞王師，而腢賊弭息矣。

translations.literal: It is fitting strictly to instruct the three high offices, to conduct secret audits of the governors, administrators, and magistrates, and wherever government is out of balance and they encroach on and abuse the common people, to memorialize at once, replace them with clean, worthy, public-spirited men who can promulgate the laws and directives in a spirit of loving kindness—then you need not weary the royal army and the banditry will subside.
translations.idiomatic: Fan urged the throne to order the three senior ministries to audit every governor and magistrate, remove the cruel and corrupt, and install humane officials who could publish kind decrees—so revolts would end without mobilizing the army.

sentences.zh: 又三署郎吏二千餘人，三府掾屬過限未除，但當擇善而授之，簡惡而去之。

translations.literal: Moreover there are more than two thousand Gentlemen of the Three Subordinate Bureaus, and the clerks and staff of the three high offices exceed their quotas and have not been dismissed; you need only choose the good and appoint them, weed out the bad and remove them.
translations.idiomatic: He reminded the emperor that over two thousand gentlemen still crowded the three palaces and that the ministries swelled with supernumeraries; promote the deserving and expel the useless, he said.

sentences.zh: 豈煩一切之詔，以長請屬之路乎！

translations.literal: Why trouble with an edict cutting across all cases, thereby lengthening the path of requests and influence?
translations.idiomatic: Why issue a blanket waiver that only widens the door to favor-seeking?

sentences.zh: 」以此忤左右，故出為豫章太守。

translations.literal: " With this he offended those about the throne, so he was sent out as Administrator of Yuzhang.
translations.idiomatic: The court favorites took offense, and Fan was sent out as governor of Yuzhang.

sentences.zh: 性方峻，不接賓客，士民亦畏其高。

translations.literal: By nature he was stern and aloof; he did not receive guests, and the gentry and commoners also stood in awe of his severity.
translations.idiomatic: Stern and aloof, he refused casual visitors, and locals learned to respect his distance.

sentences.zh: [二]征為尚書令，送者不出郭門。

translations.literal: [2] When he was summoned to be Prefect of the Masters of Writing, those who saw him off did not go beyond the outer wall of the city.
translations.idiomatic: [2] When the capital recalled him as Prefect of the Masters of Writing, not one well-wisher escorted him past the city moat.

sentences.zh: 注[一]尚書曰：「若保赤子，唯人其康乂。」

translations.literal: Note [1] The *Book of Documents* says, "If you protect the infants in arms, the people will be well governed."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] The *Book of Documents* promises that rulers who nurture their people as infants will pacify them.

sentences.zh: 注[二]蕃喪妻，鄉人畢至，唯許子將不往，曰：「仲舉性峻，峻則少通，故不造也。」

translations.literal: Note [2] When Fan lost his wife, everyone in the village came except Xu Zijiang, who said, "Zhongju's nature is severe; severity means little accommodation, so I do not go to pay my respects."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] When Fan buried his wife, the whole village attended except Xu Zijiang, who explained that Fan’s harsh temperament left little room for sociability.

sentences.zh: 遷大鴻臚。

translations.literal: He was promoted to Grand Herald.
translations.idiomatic: He advanced to Grand Herald.

sentences.zh: 會白馬令李雲抗疏諫，桓帝怒，當伏*[重]*誅。

translations.literal: It happened that the magistrate of Baima, Li Yun, presented a blunt memorial remonstrating; Emperor Huan was enraged and [Li] was sentenced to the extreme penalty.
translations.idiomatic: When Magistrate Li Yun of Baima sent a blunt memorial, Emperor Huan’s rage carried a capital sentence.

sentences.zh: 蕃上書救雲，坐免歸田裡。

translations.literal: Fan submitted a memorial to rescue Li Yun, and for that was dismissed and returned to his fields.
translations.idiomatic: Fan intervened on Li’s behalf and was stripped of office and sent home.

sentences.zh: 復征拜議郎，數日遷光祿勳。

translations.literal: He was again summoned and appointed Court Gentleman, and within a few days was promoted to Minister of the Imperial Clan.
translations.idiomatic: The court soon recalled him as a Court Gentleman and within days named him Minister of the Imperial Clan.

sentences.zh: 時封賞踰制，內寵猥盛，蕃乃上疏諫曰：「臣聞有事社稷者，社稷是為；

translations.literal: At that time enfeoffments and rewards exceeded the regulations, and favor to those inside the palace was grossly excessive, so Fan presented a memorial remonstrating, saying: "I have heard that he who has charge of the altars of soil and grain keeps those altars as his charge;
translations.idiomatic: Enfeoffments had exploded and inner-palace favorites prospered, so Fan warned: “Those who serve the altars must serve the altars—

sentences.zh: 有事人君者，容悅是為。

translations.literal: he who has charge of the ruler's person concerns himself with pleasing the ruler.
translations.idiomatic: those who serve the ruler’s person busy themselves with pleasing him.”

sentences.zh: 今臣蒙恩聖朝，備位九列，見非不諫，則容悅也。

translations.literal: Now I have received favor in this sage court and hold a place among the nine columns; if I see what is wrong and do not remonstrate, that is ingratiation.
translations.idiomatic: “Honored with a seat among the Nine Ministers, I cannot stay silent without flattering you.”

sentences.zh: 夫諸侯上象四七，垂耀在天，下應分土，藩屏上國。

translations.literal: The feudal lords above correspond to the twenty-eight lunar lodges and hang their radiance in the sky; below they match the allotted lands and screen the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Feudal lords mirror the twenty-eight mansions above and guard their allotted territories below.

sentences.zh: [二] 高祖之約，非功臣不侯。

translations.literal: [2] The compact of the Founding Emperor was: no enfeoffment as marquis except for merit.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Gaozu vowed that only merit could earn a marquisate.

sentences.zh: 而聞追錄河南尹鄧萬世父遵之微功，更爵尚書令黃鑈先人之絕封，近習以非義授邑，左右以無功傳賞，授位不料其任，裂土莫紀其功，至乃一門之內，侯者數人，故緯象失度，陰陽謬序，稼用不成，民用不康。

translations.literal: Yet I hear that you pursue and record the slight merit of Deng Zun, father of the Henan Intendant Deng Wanshi, again grant to Minister Huang Yin his ancestors' lapsed fief, while favorites receive towns without justice and attendants pass down rewards without merit; offices are given without regard to fitness, fiefs are carved without record of achievement—to the point that within a single household several men are marquises. Hence the stellar signs lose their measure, yin and yang are disordered, the harvests fail, and the people know no peace.
translations.idiomatic: Yet the throne revived petty deeds of Deng Zun, Henan governor Deng Wanshi’s father, restored Huang Yin’s family title, showered cronies with domains, and handed out salaries without talent—several marquises under one roof—until omens skewed, yin and yang slipped out of phase, harvests withered, and the people suffered.

sentences.zh: 臣知封事已行，言之無及，誠欲陛下從是而止。

translations.literal: I know the enfeoffments have already been carried out and there is no point in speaking further; I sincerely wish Your Majesty to stop here.
translations.idiomatic: Fan admitted the titles were already granted but begged the emperor to halt there.

sentences.zh: 又比年收斂，十傷五六，萬人饑寒，不聊生活，而采女數千，食肉衣綺，脂油粉黛，不可貲計。

translations.literal: Moreover in recent years levies have taken five or six victims out of ten; myriad households suffer cold and hunger and cannot live, yet there are thousands of palace women who eat meat and wear fine silk, their oils and powders beyond reckoning.
translations.idiomatic: Taxes in recent years had ruined more than half the population while thousands of harem women feasted on meat and silk and burned oil and rouge beyond counting.

sentences.zh: [二]鄙諺言『盜不過五女門』，以女貧家也。

translations.literal: [2] A vulgar proverb says, 'A thief will not pass the gate of a household with five daughters,' because daughters impoverish a family.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Folk wisdom says thieves bypass homes with five daughters because daughters beggar a house—

sentences.zh: 今後宮之女，豈不貧國乎！

translations.literal: Are not the women of the rear palace now impoverishing the state!
translations.idiomatic: yet your inner chambers now beggar the empire!”

sentences.zh: 是以傾宮嫁而天下化，[三]楚女悲而西宮災。

translations.literal: Hence when King Zhou's Tilted Palace was emptied, the realm was transformed; [3] when the Chu woman grieved, the Western Palace suffered disaster. [4]
translations.idiomatic: [3] King Zhou’s Tilted Palace was dismantled to reform the realm; [4] a neglected Chu consort’s grief fired the western palace.”

sentences.zh: [四]且聚而不御，必生憂悲之感，以致並隔水旱之困。

translations.literal: Moreover when many women are gathered but not visited, grief and resentment must arise, bringing drought and flood across neighboring regions.
translations.idiomatic: Hoarding unused concubines breeds resentment and invites drought and flood alike.

sentences.zh: 夫獄以禁止奸違，官以稱才理物。

translations.literal: Prisons exist to restrain wrongdoing; offices exist to match talent to affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Law restrains crime; office fits talent to task.

sentences.zh: 若法虧於平，官失其人，則王道有缺。

translations.literal: If the law falls short of fairness and offices lack the right men, then the royal way is flawed.
translations.idiomatic: When justice slips and the wrong men rule, the royal way cracks.

sentences.zh: 而令天下之論，皆謂獄由怨起，爵以賄成。

translations.literal: Yet the talk across the realm now is that lawsuits arise from resentment and noble rank is gained through bribes.
translations.idiomatic: Yet rumor says lawsuits spawn from spite and nobility sells to the highest bribe.

sentences.zh: 夫不有臭穢，則蒼蠅不飛。

translations.literal: Unless there is filth and stench, flies do not swarm.
translations.idiomatic: Rot draws flies.

sentences.zh: 陛下宜采求失得，擇從忠善。

translations.literal: Your Majesty ought to gather reports on failure and success and follow loyal, sound counsel.
translations.idiomatic: Fan pleaded for honest evaluations and faithful advice.

sentences.zh: 尺一選舉，委尚書三公，[五]使□責誅賞，各有所歸，豈不幸甚！

translations.literal: Let edicts written on the foot-long boards be entrusted to the Masters of Writing and the Three Dukes; [5] have them [ ] fix responsibility for blame, rewards, and punishments so that each has its proper place—would that not be most fortunate!
translations.idiomatic: [5] Let the Masters of Writing and the Three Dukes own those rescripts and the full chain of reward and punishment.

sentences.zh: 」帝頗納其言，為出宮女五百餘人，但賜鑈爵關內侯，而萬世南鄉侯。

translations.literal: " The emperor largely accepted his words, released more than five hundred palace women, but still enfeoffed Huang Yin as secondary marquis of Guannei and Deng Wanshi as marquis of Nanxiang.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor partly listened: he freed more than five hundred palace women yet still ennobled Huang Yin as a secondary marquis of Guannei and Deng Wanshi as marquis of Nanxiang.

sentences.zh: 注[一]上象四七，謂二十八宿各主諸侯之分野，故曰下應分土，言皆以輔王室也。

translations.literal: Note [1] "Above they correspond to the four sevens" means the twenty-eight lodges each govern the allotted region of a marquis; hence "below they match the allotted lands"—they all aid the royal house.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] The twenty-eight lodges govern each noble’s field; thus lords who mirror heaven also shield the capital on earth.

sentences.zh: 注[二]貲，量也。

translations.literal: Note [2] *Zi* means to measure, to reckon.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] *Zi* here means to calculate or measure.

sentences.zh: 注[三]帝王紀曰「紂作傾宮，多采美女以充之。

translations.literal: Note [3] The *Record of Emperors and Kings* says, "Zhou built the Tilted Palace and gathered many beautiful women to fill it.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3] The *Record of Emperors and Kings* describes Di Xin’s Tilted Palace stuffed with concubines.

sentences.zh: 武王伐殷，乃歸傾宮之女於諸侯」也。

translations.literal: When King Wu attacked Yin, he returned the women of the Tilted Palace to the feudal lords."
translations.idiomatic: King Wu of Zhou returned those women to the lords when he conquered Shang.”

sentences.zh: 注[四]公羊傳曰：「西宮災。

translations.literal: Note [4] The *Gongyang* commentary says, "The Western Palace caught fire."
translations.idiomatic: Note [4] The *Gongyang* chronicle records a fire in the western palace.

sentences.zh: 」何休注云：「時僖公為齊桓所脅，以齊媵為嫡，楚女廢居西宮，而不見恤，悲愁怨曠所生。」

translations.literal: He Xiu's commentary explains: "At the time Duke Xi was coerced by Duke Huan of Qi; he took a Qi woman as his principal wife and the Chu woman was cast aside in the Western Palace, untended; grief, resentment, and neglect produced it."
translations.idiomatic: He Xiu explains that Duke Xi of Lu, bullied by Duke Huan of Qi, elevated a Qi bride and abandoned his Chu consort in the western palace—her neglected sorrow fed the omen.

sentences.zh: 注[五]尺一謂板長尺一，以寫詔書也。

translations.literal: Note [5] "Foot-long" refers to boards a foot and an inch long used for writing edicts.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5] “Foot-long boards” were wooden slips about twelve inches long used for imperial rescripts.

sentences.zh: 延熹六年，車駕幸廣*

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Yanxi, the imperial train visited Guang—
translations.idiomatic: In Yanxi 6 the emperor traveled to Guang—

translations.literal: (cheng)
translations.idiomatic: (—the syllable cheng, completing the place-name Guangcheng—)

translations.literal: —[cheng] for a hunt on the training ground.
translations.idiomatic: —to Guangcheng—for a formal hunt on the training ground.

sentences.zh: [一]蕃上疏諫曰：「臣聞人君有事於苑囿，唯仲秋西郊，順時講武，殺禽助祭，以敦孝敬。

translations.literal: [1] Fan presented a memorial remonstrating, saying: "I have heard that when a ruler has business in the parks and hunting reserves, it is only in mid-autumn at the western suburban altar, in accord with the season, that he practices military exercises, kills game to assist the sacrifice, and thereby deepens filial respect.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Fan remonstrated: “Rulers enter the hunting parks at most in mid-autumn at the western suburb, timing martial drills with the season, killing game to assist sacrifice and reinforce filial duty.

sentences.zh: 如或違此，則為肆縱。

translations.literal: If he departs from this, it is wanton indulgence.
translations.idiomatic: Anything else is dissipation.”

sentences.zh: 故□陶戒舜『無教逸游』，[二]周公戒成王『無盤於游田』。

translations.literal: Hence Gao Yao admonished Shun, "Do not teach dissolute wandering," [2] and the Duke of Zhou admonished King Cheng, "Do not take your pleasure in hunting and field sports."
translations.idiomatic: [2] Gao Yao had cautioned Shun about dissolute wandering, and the Duke of Zhou had cautioned King Cheng about sport in the fields.

sentences.zh: [三]虞舜、成王猶有此戒，況德不及二主者乎！

translations.literal: [3] Even Shun and King Cheng had such warnings—how much more one whose virtue does not match those two lords!
translations.idiomatic: [3] If even those models needed the warning, what of a lesser ruler?

sentences.zh: 夫安平之時，尚宜有節，況當今之世，有三空之□哉！

translations.literal: In times of peace restraint is still proper; how much more in the present age, when there is peril [ ] amid the three voids!
translations.idiomatic: Restraint suits even peacetime—let alone an age gutted by the “three voids” and this added danger.

sentences.zh: 田野空，朝廷空，倉庫空，是謂三空。

translations.literal: The fields are void, the court is void, the granaries are void—this is called the three voids.
translations.idiomatic: Farmland lies waste, the court stands empty, granaries gape—those are the three voids.

sentences.zh: 加兵戎未戢，四方離散，是陛下焦心毀顏，坐以待旦之時也。

translations.literal: Add to that arms not yet laid down and the four quarters scattered in flight—this is a time when Your Majesty should be anxious to the point of wasting away, sitting through the night awaiting dawn.
translations.idiomatic: Arms are still up, the realm in flight; you should be sleepless with care, not parading for sport.

sentences.zh: 豈宜揚旗曜武，騁心輿馬之觀乎！

translations.literal: How could it be fitting to raise banners and display arms, to give your heart over to the spectacle of chariots and horses!
translations.idiomatic: How can it be fitting to raise banners and revel in chariots and horses?

sentences.zh: 又*

translations.literal: Moreover—
translations.idiomatic: Moreover—

translations.literal: (earlier)
translations.idiomatic: (earlier)

sentences.zh: *秋*[前]*多雨，民始種麥。

translations.literal: —early autumn had much rain; the people had just begun to plant wheat.
translations.idiomatic: —early autumn brought heavy rains just as farmers sowed wheat.

sentences.zh: 今失其勸種之時，而令給驅禽除路之役，非賢聖恤民之意也。

translations.literal: Now you miss the season that urges planting and instead put them to driving game and clearing roads—not the mind of the sages who pity the people.
translations.idiomatic: To interrupt planting for beaters and road crews is not how sages show mercy to the people.

sentences.zh: 齊景公欲觀於海，放乎琅邪，晏子為陳百姓惡聞旌旗輿馬之音，舉首嚬眉之感，景公為之不行。

translations.literal: Duke Jing of Qi wished to view the sea and roam to Langya; Yanzi set before him how the people loathed the sound of banners, chariots, and horses, and the feeling of raised brows and knitted foreheads, and Duke Jing therefore did not go.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Jing of Qi wanted to tour the sea toward Langya; Yanzi described the people’s dread of war equipage until the duke abandoned the trip.

sentences.zh: 周穆王欲肆車轍馬跡，祭公謀父為誦祈招之詩，以止其心。

translations.literal: King Mu of Zhou wished to run his chariot tracks and horse hoofs to every corner; Duke Zhai the Elder recited for him the poem "Qi zhao" to rein in his heart.
translations.idiomatic: King Mu of Zhou meant to race to the ends of the earth until Duke Zhai recited “Qi zhao” to bridle him.

sentences.zh: 誠惡逸游之害人也。

translations.literal: He truly feared the harm that dissolute wandering does to people.
translations.idiomatic: Those stories illustrate how ruinous heedless touring can be.

sentences.zh: 」[四] 書奏不納。

translations.literal: " [4] The memorial was presented but not accepted.
translations.idiomatic: [4] The memorial went in but was not accepted.

sentences.zh: 注[一]廣*

translations.literal: Note [1] Guang—
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] Guang—

translations.literal: (cheng)
translations.idiomatic: (the syllable “cheng”)

sentences.zh: **[成]*，苑名，在今汝州梁縣西也。

translations.literal: —[cheng] is the name of a park; it lies west of Liang County in modern Ruzhou.
translations.idiomatic: —forms Guangcheng, the name of the hunt park west of Liang County in modern Ruzhou.

sentences.zh: 注[二]尚書咎繇謨曰：「無教逸欲有邦。」

translations.literal: Note [2] The *Book of Documents*, "Counsels of Gao Yao," says, "Do not teach dissolute desires in the states."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] The *Counsels of Gao Yao* tells rulers not to encourage dissipation in the states.

sentences.zh: 注[三]尚書無逸篇之言。

translations.literal: Note [3] This is a saying from the "Wu Yi" chapter of the *Book of Documents*.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3] The line comes from the *Book of Documents*, "Wu Yi."

sentences.zh: 注[四]祭公，祭國公，為周卿士。

translations.literal: Note [4] Duke Zhai was the duke of the state of Zhai and served Zhou as a high minister.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4] “Duke Zhai” was ruler of the Zhai polity and a ranking minister at the Zhou court.

sentences.zh: 謀父，名也。

translations.literal: Moufu was his personal name.
translations.idiomatic: His given name was Moufu.

sentences.zh: 祈招，逸詩也。

translations.literal: "Qi zhao" is a lost poem from the classic corpus.
translations.idiomatic: “Qi zhao” survives only as a quotation—a lost ode.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「昔周穆王欲肆其心，周行天下，將皆必有車轍馬跡焉。

translations.literal: The *Zuo Tradition* says: "In ancient times King Mu of Zhou wished to give free rein to his heart and travel throughout the realm; he meant for chariot tracks and horse hoofs to reach everywhere."
translations.idiomatic: The *Zuo Tradition* records King Mu’s plan to indulge his wanderlust until cart ruts and hoofprints covered the world.

sentences.zh: 祭公謀父作祈招之詩以止王心。

translations.literal: Duke Zhai the Elder composed the poem "Qi zhao" to check the king’s desires.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Zhai composed “Qi zhao” to rein in the king’s appetite for travel.

sentences.zh: 其詩曰：『祈招之愔愔，式昭德音，思我王度，式如玉，式如金。

translations.literal: The poem says: "How gentle is the prayer of *qi zhao*; it displays virtuous reputation; think of our king’s measure—like jade, like gold.
translations.idiomatic: The verses run: how soft the prayer of *qi zhao*; it proclaims a king’s virtuous name; mind the royal standard—firm as jade, firm as gold.

sentences.zh: 刑人之力，而無醉飽之心。

translations.literal: It takes the strength of the people but harbors no heart of drunken surfeit."
translations.idiomatic: It uses the people’s strength yet never feasts to excess on power.”

sentences.zh: 』」自蕃為光祿勳，與五官中郎將黃琬共典選舉，不偏權富，而為埶家郎所譖訴，坐免歸。

translations.literal: After Fan became Minister of the Imperial Clan, he shared supervision of nominations with the General of the Household for All Purposes, Huang Wan; they showed no partiality to the powerful or wealthy, and so the sons of influential houses slandered them; [Fan] was dismissed and sent home for it.
translations.idiomatic: Once Fan served as Minister of the Imperial Clan alongside Huang Wan as joint overseer of appointments, their impartiality provoked powerful families to lodge complaints, and Fan lost his post.

sentences.zh: 頃之，征為尚僕射，轉太中大夫。

translations.literal: Before long he was summoned as Supervisor of the Masters of Writing and then transferred to Palace Grandee.
translations.idiomatic: Soon the court recalled him as Supervisor of the Masters of Writing and moved him up to Grand Palace Grandee.

sentences.zh: 八年，代楊秉為太尉。

translations.literal: In the eighth year [of Yanxi] he replaced Yang Bing as Grand Commandant.
translations.idiomatic: In Yanxi 8 he succeeded Yang Bing as Grand Commandant.

sentences.zh: 蕃讓曰：「『不愆不忘，率由舊章，』[一]臣不如太常胡廣。

translations.literal: Fan declined, saying: "'Do not err and do not forget; follow the ancient models'—[1] in this I am not the equal of Minister of Ceremonies Hu Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Fan tried to decline: “‘Commit no fault, forget nothing—hold to the old statutes’—[1] here I cannot match Minister Hu Guang.”

sentences.zh: 齊七政，訓五典，臣不如議郎王暢。

translations.literal: "To align the seven regulators and expound the five canons, I am not the equal of Court Gentleman Wang Chang.
translations.idiomatic: “To align the seven regulators and expound the five canons, I fall short of Court Gentleman Wang Chang.”

sentences.zh: 聰明亮達，文武兼姿，臣不如□刑徒李膺。

translations.literal: In keen intelligence, radiant insight, and combining civil and military gifts, I am not the equal of the convict laborer Li Ying."
translations.idiomatic: “In clarity of mind and fusion of civil and military talent, I am no match for Li Ying, even under sentence.”

sentences.zh: 」帝不許。

translations.literal: The emperor did not permit [his resignation].
translations.idiomatic: The emperor refused to let him step down.

sentences.zh: 注[一]詩大雅也。

translations.literal: Note [1] These lines are from the *Book of Songs*, "Great Odes."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] The quotation comes from the *Book of Songs*, *Greater Odes*.

sentences.zh: 言成王令德，不過誤，不遺失，循用舊典文章，謂周公之禮法也。

translations.literal: It speaks of King Cheng’s excellent virtue—without error, without lapse—following the old statutes and patterns, meaning the rites and laws of the Duke of Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss praises King Cheng for governing without misstep, adhering to the inherited ritual code—the regulations left by the Duke of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 中常侍蘇康、管霸等復被任用，遂排陷忠良，共相阿媚。

translations.literal: The Regular Palace Attendants Su Kang and Guan Ba and others were again employed; they thereupon slandered and trapped the loyal and good, flattering one another.
translations.idiomatic: Eunuchs such as Su Kang and Guan Ba regained power, ousted honest officials, and traded favors among themselves.

sentences.zh: 大司農劉佑、廷尉馮緄、[一]河南尹李膺，皆以忤旨，為之抵罪。

translations.literal: The Minister of Agriculture Liu You, Commandant of Justice Feng Gun, [1] and Henan Governor Li Ying all offended the imperial intent and suffered punishment on that account.
translations.idiomatic: Minister of Agriculture Liu You, Commandant of Justice Feng Gun, [1] and Henan Governor Li Ying each crossed the throne and paid for it.

sentences.zh: 蕃因朝會，固理膺等，請加原宥，升之爵任。

translations.literal: At the court assembly Fan firmly argued their case, asking that they be pardoned and promoted to offices with noble rank.
translations.idiomatic: At audience Fan pressed their cause, seeking pardons and restored honors.

sentences.zh: 言及反覆，誠辭懇切。

translations.literal: He spoke back and forth with earnest, sincere language.
translations.idiomatic: He pleaded in earnest, again and again.

sentences.zh: 帝不聽，因流涕而起。

translations.literal: The emperor would not listen, so [Fan] rose with tears streaming.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor turned a deaf ear; Fan rose in tears.

sentences.zh: 時小黃門趙津、南陽大猾張*

translations.literal: At that time the Junior Yellow Gate Zhao Jin, and the great scoundrel of Nanyang Zhang—
translations.idiomatic: Around then the Junior Yellow Gate Zhao Jin and Nanyang’s notorious Zhang—

translations.literal: (si)

sentences.zh: **[泛]*等，奉事中官，乘埶犯法，二郡太守劉□、成□考案其罪，雖經赦令，而並竟考殺之。

translations.literal: —[fan] and others, serving the inner attendants, abused their power and broke the law; the two commandery governors Liu [ ], Cheng [ ] investigated their crimes and, although amnesty had been proclaimed, in the end interrogated and executed them.
translations.idiomatic: —completing Zhang Fan—exploited eunuch patrons until Governors Liu and Cheng of the two commanderies tried and executed them despite an amnesty.

sentences.zh: 宦官怨恚，有司承旨，遂奏□、□罪當□市。

translations.literal: The eunuchs bore a grudge; the responsible offices, carrying out secret intent, therefore memorialized that Liu [ ] and Cheng [ ] deserved execution in the marketplace.
translations.idiomatic: The eunuchs seethed; compliant agencies memorialized that both governors merited public execution.

sentences.zh: 又山陽太守翟超，沒入中常侍侯覽財產，東海相黃浮，誅殺下邳令徐宣，超、浮並坐髡鉗，輸作左校。

translations.literal: Moreover Zhai Chao, governor of Shanyang, confiscated the property of Regular Palace Attendant Hou Lan; Huang Fu, chancellor of Donghai, executed the magistrate of Xiapi, Xu Xuan; Chao and Fu were both sentenced to shave their heads, wear the collar and fetters, and be sent to hard labor in the Left Workshop.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Chao of Shanyang seized Hou Lan’s estates; Huang Fu of Donghai executed Magistrate Xu Xuan of Xiapi; both were shaved, collared, and sent to the Left Workshop.

sentences.zh: 蕃與司徒劉矩、司空劉茂共諫請□、□、超、浮等，帝不悅。

translations.literal: Fan together with Minister of Education Liu Ju and Minister of Works Liu Mao jointly remonstrated, asking pardon for Liu [ ], Cheng [ ], Chao, and Fu; the emperor was displeased.
translations.idiomatic: Fan, Liu Ju, and Liu Mao jointly pleaded for all four; the emperor bristled.

sentences.zh: 有司劾奏之，矩、茂不敢復言。

translations.literal: The responsible offices impeached them; Ju and Mao dared speak no further.
translations.idiomatic: Impeachment followed; Ju and Mao fell silent.

sentences.zh: 蕃乃獨上疏曰：「臣聞齊桓修霸，務為內政；

translations.literal: Fan thereupon presented a lone memorial, saying: "I have heard that when Duke Huan of Qi cultivated hegemony, he devoted himself to internal government;
translations.idiomatic: Fan then submitted a solitary memorial: “Duke Huan of Qi built hegemony by putting domestic rule first;

sentences.zh: [二]春秋於魯，小惡必書。

translations.literal: [2] The *Spring and Autumn*, for Lu, records even small wrongs.
translations.idiomatic: [2] For Lu, the *Spring and Autumn* notes even petty offenses.”

sentences.zh: [三]宜先自整□，後以及人。

translations.literal: It is proper first to set one’s own house in order, then to extend it to others.
translations.idiomatic: Put your own house in order before lecturing the world.”

sentences.zh: 今寇賊在外，四支之疾；

translations.literal: Now bandits are outside—the ailment of the four limbs;
translations.idiomatic: Rebels at the border are an ailment of the limbs;

sentences.zh: 內政不理，心腹之患。

translations.literal: internal government neglected is the malady of the heart and belly.
translations.idiomatic: Neglected domestic rule rots the heart and belly.”

sentences.zh: 臣寢不能寐，食不能飽，實憂左右日親，忠言以疏，內患漸積，外難方深。

translations.literal: Your servant cannot sleep at night or eat his fill at meals, truly fearing that those at Your Majesty’s side grow daily more intimate, loyal counsel grows remote, internal troubles accumulate, and external peril deepens.
translations.idiomatic: I lie awake and cannot eat, fearing that favorites crowd you while honest words fade, inner rot spreads, and foreign threats swell.”

sentences.zh: 陛下超從列侯，繼承天位。

translations.literal: Your Majesty leapt from being a full marquis to inheriting the heavenly throne.
translations.idiomatic: You vaulted from enfeoffed marquis to Son of Heaven.”

sentences.zh: [四]小家畜產百萬之資，子孫尚恥愧失其先業，況乃產兼天下，受之先帝，而欲懈怠以自輕忽乎？

translations.literal: [4] A petty household that possesses assets worth a million, its sons and grandsons are still ashamed to lose the founder’s enterprise—how much more when the estate embraces all under heaven, received from the late emperor, yet you would slacken and treat it lightly?
translations.idiomatic: [4] Even a family worth a million mourns a lost legacy—how can you who inherited the realm from the late emperor treat it lightly?”

sentences.zh: 誠不愛己，不當念先帝得之勤苦邪？

translations.literal: If you truly do not cherish yourself, ought you not recall how painfully the late emperor won it?
translations.idiomatic: If you will not care for yourself, remember the late emperor’s labor.”

sentences.zh: 前梁氏五族，毒□海內，[五]天啟聖意，收而戮之，天下之議，冀當小平。

translations.literal: Formerly the five clans of the Liang poisoned [ ] within the seas; [5] Heaven awakened the sage intent, gathered them in and executed them; discussion across the realm hoped for a little peace.
translations.idiomatic: The five Liang houses once poisoned the realm until Heaven inspired their purge—everyone hoped calm would follow.”

sentences.zh: 明鑒未遠，覆車如昨，而近習之權，復相扇結。

translations.literal: The bright mirror is not far off; the overturned chariot was like yesterday—yet the power of those close at hand again knots itself together.
translations.idiomatic: That lesson is still warm, yet court favorites entwine again.”

sentences.zh: 小黃門趙津、大猾張*

translations.literal: The Junior Yellow Gate Zhao Jin, the great scoundrel Zhang—
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Jin and the flagrant Zhang—

translations.literal: (si)
translations.idiomatic: (si)

sentences.zh: **[泛]*等，肆行貪虐，奸媚左右，前太原太守劉□、南陽太守成□，糾而戮之。

translations.literal: —[fan] and others, acting wildly in greed and cruelty, flattered those about the throne; the former governor of Taiyuan Liu [ ], the governor of Nanyang Cheng [ ], investigated and executed them.
translations.idiomatic: —Zhang Fan—waxed cruel and obsequious until Governors Liu and Cheng of Taiyuan and Nanyang struck them down.”

sentences.zh: 雖言赦後不當誅殺，原其誠心，在乎去惡。

translations.literal: Although it is said that after an amnesty they ought not to have been put to death, their sincere intent lay in removing evil.
translations.idiomatic: Even if killing them breached the amnesty, their aim was to cut out corruption.”

sentences.zh: 至於陛下，有何悁悁？

translations.literal: As for Your Majesty, what resentment could there be?
translations.idiomatic: What grudge could you hold?”

sentences.zh: [六]而小人道長，營惑聖聽，遂使天威為之發怒。

translations.literal: [6] Yet petty men’s ways lengthen; they encircle and delude the sage hearing, so that heavenly majesty is made to rage on their behalf.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Yet petty men thrive, cloud your ears, and stir your wrath for them.”

sentences.zh: 如加刑□，已為過甚，況乃重罰，令伏歐刀乎！

translations.literal: If corporal punishment were added, it would already be excessive—how much more a heavy sentence to make them fall beneath the executioner’s blade!
translations.idiomatic: Corporal punishment would already be harsh—how much worse the headsman’s axe!”

sentences.zh: 又前山陽太守翟超、東海相黃浮，奉公不橈，疾惡如讎，超沒侯覽財物，浮誅徐宣之罪，並蒙刑坐，不逢赦恕，覽之從橫，沒財已幸；

translations.literal: Moreover the former governors Zhai Chao of Shanyang and Huang Fu of Donghai held firm for the public good and did not bend; they hated evil like an enemy; Chao confiscated Hou Lan’s goods; Fu punished Xu Xuan’s crime; both suffered penal labor without receiving amnesty or forgiveness; Lan’s wanton conduct—confiscation of property was already fortune enough;
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Chao and Huang Fu served the public and hated villainy: Zhai seized Hou Lan’s wealth; Huang punished Xu Xuan. Both were punished without mercy while Hou Lan still lorded it—seizing his goods was luck enough;

sentences.zh: 宣犯釁過，死有餘辜。

translations.literal: Xuan’s offense and guilt deserved death with surplus.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan’s crimes merited death ten times over.”

sentences.zh: 昔丞相申屠嘉召責鄧通，洛陽令董宣折辱公主，而文帝從而請之，光武加以重賞，[七]未聞二臣有專命之誅。

translations.literal: In former times Chancellor Shen Tu Jia summoned and rebuked Deng Tong; the Luoyang magistrate Dong Xuan humiliated a princess, yet Emperor Wen followed up by speaking for them, and Emperor Guangwu gave them heavy reward—[7] I have not heard that those two ministers suffered execution on their own authority.
translations.idiomatic: Shen Tu Jia humiliated Deng Tong; Dong Xuan shamed a princess—yet Han Wen and Guangwu rewarded such spine; neither minister was executed for acting alone.”

sentences.zh: 而今左右腢豎，惡傷黨類，妄相交構，致此刑譴。

translations.literal: Yet now the favorites about the throne, hating harm to their party, fabricate charges against one another, bringing about these punishments and reproofs.
translations.idiomatic: Today palace toadies, nursing factional spite, invent crimes and invite harsh verdicts.”

sentences.zh: 聞臣是言，當復□訴。

translations.literal: When they hear your servant’s words, they will surely [ ] lodge complaints again.
translations.idiomatic: They will answer with fresh slander.”

sentences.zh: 陛下深宜割塞近習豫政之源，引納尚書朝省之事，公卿大官，五日壹朝，[八]簡練清高，斥黜佞邪。

translations.literal: Your Majesty ought deeply to cut off and block the sources by which those close at hand anticipate government; admit the business of the Masters of Writing and morning court; let the senior ministers, once every five days, [8] hold audience—select the pure and lofty, dismiss the flattering and wicked.
translations.idiomatic: Cut favorites out of policy; restore audiences so high ministers meet every five days [8] while you elevate the upright and expel sycophants.”

sentences.zh: 如是天和於上，地洽於下，休禎符瑞，豈遠乎哉！

translations.literal: If so, heaven will be harmonious above and earth concordant below—would auspicious omens be far off!
translations.idiomatic: Do that and harmony returns—auspices cannot lag.”

sentences.zh: 陛下雖厭毒臣言，凡人主有自勉強，敢以死陳。

translations.literal: Although Your Majesty detests your servant’s words, any lord may force himself to improvement; I dare present this though it mean death.
translations.idiomatic: Though my counsel disgusts you, a ruler may yet steel himself—I risk death to say it.”

sentences.zh: 」帝得奏愈怒，竟無所納。

translations.literal: " The emperor, receiving the memorial, grew angrier still and in the end adopted none of it.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial only deepened the emperor’s rage; he adopted nothing.

sentences.zh: 朝廷觿庶莫不怨之。

translations.literal: There was none among the court officials and the multitude who did not resent him.
translations.idiomatic: Officialdom seethed at him.

sentences.zh: 宦官由此疾蕃彌甚，選舉奏議，輒以中詔譴卻，長*

translations.literal: The eunuchs therefore hated Fan all the more; whenever nominations or memorials were discussed, they would reject them with edicts from the inner palace, and long—
translations.idiomatic: Eunuchs loathed Fan more each day, quashing nominations with palace edicts, while long—

translations.literal: (li)

sentences.zh: **[史]*已下多至抵罪。

translations.literal: —[shi] clerks and below were often made to answer for crimes.
translations.idiomatic: —completing *shi* for junior clerks—often paid the price.

sentences.zh: 猶以蕃名臣，不敢加害。

translations.literal: Still, because Fan was a famous minister, they did not dare harm him.
translations.idiomatic: They still feared to touch so eminent a minister.

sentences.zh: □字文理，高唐人。

translations.literal: [ ]’s courtesy name was Wenli; he was a man of Gaotang.
translations.idiomatic: The first governor’s [ ] style was Wenli; he came from Gaotang.

sentences.zh: [九]□字幼平，陝人。

translations.literal: [9] [ ]’s courtesy name was Youping; he was a man of Shan.
translations.idiomatic: [9] The second governor’s [ ] style was Youping; he hailed from Shan.

sentences.zh: 並有經術稱，處位敢直言，多所搏擊，知名當時，皆死於獄中。

translations.literal: Both had a reputation for classical learning, dared speak bluntly in office, and struck at many targets; they were renowned in their time but all died in prison.
translations.idiomatic: Both were classical scholars, outspoken in office, and combative; celebrated in life, they died in jail.

sentences.zh: 注[一]音古本反。

translations.literal: Note [1] The pronunciation follows the *gu-ben* reading.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] Gloss gives the *fanqie* reading *gu-ben*.

sentences.zh: 注[二]國語曰：「桓帝問管仲曰：『安國可乎？

translations.literal: Note [2] The *Discourses of the States* says: "Emperor Huan asked Guan Zhong, 'Can we stabilize the state?'
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] The *Discourses* records Duke Huan asking Guan Zhong whether the state could be secured.

sentences.zh: 』對曰：『未可。

translations.literal: " He answered, 'Not yet.
translations.idiomatic: Guan answered, “Not yet.”

sentences.zh: 君若正卒伍，修甲兵，大國亦如之。

translations.literal: If you, my lord, set the squads in order and repair armor and weapons, the great states will do the same.
translations.idiomatic: “If you drill troops and stock arms, rival powers will match you.”

sentences.zh: 若欲速得志於天下諸侯，則可以隱令，可以寄政。

translations.literal: If you wish quickly to gain your will among the feudal lords of the realm, you may use hidden orders; you may lodge government [elsewhere].'
translations.idiomatic: “To win the feudal lords quickly, you may issue secret orders and delegate government.”

sentences.zh: 』公曰：

translations.literal: " The duke said:
translations.idiomatic: The duke asked,

sentences.zh: 『隱令寄政若何？

translations.literal: "What of lodging government through hidden orders?"
translations.idiomatic: “What does it mean to delegate through secret orders?”

sentences.zh: 』對曰：『作內政而寄軍令焉。

translations.literal: " He answered: 'Make internal government and lodge military command within it."'
translations.idiomatic: Guan said, “Build domestic institutions and fold military command into them.”

sentences.zh: 』」注[三]公羊傳莊公四年，公及齊人狩於郜，譏其與讎狩也。

translations.literal: " Note [3] The *Gongyang* commentary, fourth year of Duke Zhuang: the duke and the men of Qi hunted in Gao—the text criticizes hunting with an enemy.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3] *Gongyang* on Zhuang 4 criticizes a joint hunt with Qi as hunting with a foe.

sentences.zh: 僖公二十年，新作南門，譏其奢也。

translations.literal: In the twentieth year of Duke Xi, the new south gate was built—the text criticizes its extravagance.
translations.idiomatic: Xi 20’s new south gate is faulted for extravagance.

sentences.zh: 故曰「小惡必書」也。

translations.literal: Hence it says "even small wrongs must be recorded."
translations.idiomatic: Hence the line “small wrongs are always written down.”

sentences.zh: 注[四]言桓帝以蠡吾侯即位。

translations.literal: Note [4] It means that Emperor Huan ascended from being marquis of Liwu.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4] The gloss notes Huan’s rise from the Liwu marquisate.

sentences.zh: 注[五]五侯謂胤、讓、淑、忠、戟五人，與冀同時誅。

translations.literal: Note [5] The five marquises mean Yin, Rang, Shu, Zhong, and Ji—five men executed together with [Liang] Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5] The “five marquises” are Yin, Rang, Shu, Zhong, and Ji—executed with Liang Ji.

sentences.zh: 事見冀傳也。

translations.literal: The affair is seen in Ji’s biography.
translations.idiomatic: See Liang Ji’s biography for detail.

sentences.zh: 注[六]說文曰：「悁悁，恚忿。」

translations.literal: Note [6] The *Shuowen* says: "Yuan yuan means resentful anger."
translations.idiomatic: Note [6] *Shuowen* defines *yuan yuan* as smoldering resentment.

sentences.zh: 注[七]文帝時，太中大夫鄧通愛幸，居上旁有怠嫚禮。

translations.literal: Note [7] In Emperor Wen’s time the Palace Grandee Deng Tong enjoyed favor; seated beside the emperor he grew negligent of ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Note [7] Under Wendi the favorite Deng Tong lounged beside the throne and grew negligent.

sentences.zh: 氶相申屠嘉入朝，因見之，為檄召通。

translations.literal: Chancellor Shen Tu Jia entered court, saw him, and issued a summons to Tong through a written order.
translations.idiomatic: Shen Tu Jia entered court, saw him, and summoned Deng by writ.

sentences.zh: 通至，嘉曰：「通小臣，戲殿上，大不敬，當斬。

translations.literal: When Tong arrived, Jia said: "Tong is a petty minister who frolicked in the hall—great irreverence; he should be beheaded.
translations.idiomatic: When Deng arrived, Shen said, “A petty favorite who capers in the hall commits grave irreverence—he should die.”

sentences.zh: 」通頓首，首盡出血。

translations.literal: " Tong knocked his head until it bled.
translations.idiomatic: Deng kowtowed until his head bled.

sentences.zh: 文帝使使召通，而謝丞相曰「吾弄臣，君釋之」也。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen sent a messenger to summon Tong and apologized to the chancellor, saying "He is my jester; release him."
translations.idiomatic: Wendi recalled Deng and told the chancellor, “He is my plaything—let him go.”

sentences.zh: 湖陽公主蒼頭白日殺人，匿主家，吏追不得。

translations.literal: The Princess of Huyang’s slave killed a man in broad daylight and hid in the princess’s house; the officials could not catch him.
translations.idiomatic: A Huyang princess’s slave murdered in daylight and hid on her estate beyond reach.

sentences.zh: 公主出，宣駐車叩馬，以刀畫地數主。

translations.literal: When the princess went out, Xuan halted his chariot, struck the horse, and with his knife drew a line on the ground, rebuking the princess.
translations.idiomatic: When she rode out, Magistrate Dong Xuan stopped the train, drew a line in the dust, and rebuked her.

sentences.zh: 主言於帝，帝賜宣錢三十萬。

translations.literal: The princess spoke to the emperor, and the emperor gave Xuan three hundred thousand cash.
translations.idiomatic: She complained; Guangwu paid Dong three hundred thousand cash.

sentences.zh: 語見董宣傳。

translations.literal: The language is seen in Dong Xuan’s biography.
translations.idiomatic: Details appear in Dong Xuan’s biography.

sentences.zh: 注[八]宣帝五日一聽事，自丞相已下，各敷奏其言。

translations.literal: Note [8] Emperor Xuan held court once every five days; from the chancellor downward each set forth his words.
translations.idiomatic: Note [8] Xuandi heard business every five days as ministers reported in turn.

sentences.zh: 注[九]高唐，縣名，今博州縣也。

translations.literal: Note [9] Gaotang is a county name—today’s county in Bozhou.
translations.idiomatic: Note [9] Gaotang county lies in modern Bozhou.

sentences.zh: 九年，李膺等以黨事下獄考實。

translations.literal: In the ninth year, Li Ying and others were cast into prison over the faction case and examined.
translations.idiomatic: In year 9 Li Ying and company entered prison as the “faction” affair was investigated.

sentences.zh: 蕃因上疏極諫曰：「臣聞賢明之君，委心輔佐；

translations.literal: Fan therefore presented a memorial remonstrating forcefully, saying: "I have heard that worthy and enlightened rulers entrust their hearts to assistants;
translations.idiomatic: Fan sent an urgent memorial: “Wise rulers lean on good ministers;

sentences.zh: 亡國之主，諱聞直辭。

translations.literal: lords who lose the state shun straight words.
translations.idiomatic: ruined thrones dread blunt counsel.”

sentences.zh: 故湯武雖聖，而興於伊呂；

translations.literal: Thus though Tang and Wu were sages, they rose through Yi Yin and Lü [Wang];
translations.idiomatic: Tang and Wu owed Yi Yin and Lü Wang;

sentences.zh: 桀紂迷惑，亡在失人。

translations.literal: Jie and Zhou were deluded, and perished through losing the right men.
translations.idiomatic: Jie and Zhou fell because they lost good men.”

sentences.zh: [一] 由此言之，君為元首，臣為股肱，同體相須，共成美惡者也。

translations.literal: [1] From this one may say: the ruler is the head; the ministers are the arms and thighs; they share one body and depend on one another, together forming good and ill.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Ruler and minister are head and limbs—one body sharing glory or shame.”

sentences.zh: [二]伏見前司隸校尉李膺、太僕杜密、太尉掾范滂等，正身無玷，死心社稷。

translations.literal: [2] I humbly observe that the former Metropolitan Superintendent Li Ying, Minister of the Household Du Mi, and junior clerk of the Grand Commandant Fan Pang kept themselves without blemish and gave their death for the altars of soil and grain.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Li Ying, Du Mi, and Fan Pang were stainless and gave their lives for the state.”

sentences.zh: 以忠忤旨，橫加考案，或禁錮閉隔，或死徙非所。

translations.literal: Because of loyalty they offended the intent; they were subjected to cruel examination; some were shut away and isolated; some died or were banished to improper places.
translations.idiomatic: Loyalty earned them torture—some immured, some slain or banished.

sentences.zh: 杜塞天下之口，聾盲一世之人，與秦焚書坑儒，何以為異？

translations.literal: To stop up the mouths of all under heaven and deafen and blind a generation of men—how does it differ from Qin’s burning the books and burying the scholars?
translations.idiomatic: Gagging the realm blinds an age no less than Qin’s burning of books and burying of scholars.”

sentences.zh: [三]昔武王克殷，表閭封墓，[四]今陛下臨政，先誅忠賢。

translations.literal: [3] In olden days King Wu conquered Yin, marked the gate of the worthy and sealed [Bi Gan’s] tomb; [4] now Your Majesty holds court and begins by executing the loyal and worthy.
translations.idiomatic: [3] King Wu honored virtue after conquering Shang; [4] you open court by killing the loyal.”

sentences.zh: 遇善何薄？

translations.literal: Why do you treat the good so meanly?
translations.idiomatic: Why slight good men?

sentences.zh: 待惡何優？

translations.literal: Why do you treat the wicked so generously?
translations.idiomatic: Why indulge evil men?

sentences.zh: 夫讒人似實，巧言如簧，[五]使聽之者惑，視之者昏。

translations.literal: Slanderers seem truthful; their clever words are like reed pipes; [5] they make those who hear confused and those who look on dim.
translations.idiomatic: Slander sounds plausible and tongues wag like panpipes [5]—enough to bewilder any ear or eye.

sentences.zh: 夫吉凶之□，存乎識善；

translations.literal: As for the [ ] of fortune and misfortune, it lies in recognizing the good.
translations.idiomatic: Weal and woe turn on whether you can tell good from ill.

sentences.zh: 成敗之機，在於察言。

translations.literal: The pivot of success and failure lies in judging what is said.
translations.idiomatic: Victory or defeat hangs on how you read men’s words.

sentences.zh: 人君者，攝天地之政，秉四海之維，舉動不可以違聖法，進退不可以離道規。

translations.literal: The lord of men takes in hand the governance of Heaven and Earth and holds the cords of the four seas; in movement he may not violate sage law, in advance or retreat he may not leave the norms of the Way.
translations.idiomatic: A ruler wields authority over heaven and earth and the four seas—every step must follow the sages, every move stay within the Way.

sentences.zh: 謬言出口，則亂及八方，何況髡無罪於獄，殺無辜於市乎！

translations.literal: When false words leave the mouth, disorder reaches the eight directions—how much more when you shave the guiltless in prison and slay the innocent in the marketplace!
translations.idiomatic: A single lie can shake the realm—let alone torturing the innocent in jail and executing them in the square!

sentences.zh: 昔禹巡狩蒼梧，見市殺人，下車而哭之曰：『萬方有罪，在予一人！

translations.literal: In olden days, when Yu toured on inspection to Cangwu, he saw someone killed in the market; he left his carriage and wept, saying, "If any among the myriad regions is guilty, the fault is in me alone!"
translations.idiomatic: When Yu traveled to Cangwu and saw an execution in the market, he left his chariot and wept that all guilt under heaven rested on him alone.

sentences.zh: 』故其興也勃焉。

translations.literal: " Hence his rise was swift and strong.
translations.idiomatic: That self-blame is why his house rose so fast.

sentences.zh: [六]又青、徐炎旱，五穀損傷，民物流遷，茹菽不足。

translations.literal: [6] Moreover Qing and Xu have suffered burning drought; the five grains are damaged; the people drift in flight; they cannot get even beans to eat.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Qing and Xu are scorched; crops fail; refugees wander; they cannot fill their bellies with beans.

sentences.zh: [七]而宮女積於房掖，國用盡於羅紈，外戚私門，貪財受賂，所謂『祿去公室，政在大夫』。

translations.literal: [7] Yet palace women crowd the inner chambers, the state’s wealth is poured into gauze and silk, affines and private houses take bribes—what the *Analects* calls "stipends left the ducal house; government passed to the great officers."
translations.idiomatic: [7] Meanwhile the harem swells, the treasury bleeds into silks, in-laws take bribes—the classic case of “revenue left the court and policy moved to great clans.”

sentences.zh: [八]昔春秋之末，周德衰微，數十年閒無復災眚者，天所□也。

translations.literal: [8] At the end of the *Spring and Autumn* era Zhou virtue withered; for decades no disasters appeared—Heaven [ ] .
translations.idiomatic: [8] Late Zhou decay saw Heaven withhold calamities for decades—

sentences.zh: [九]天之於漢，悢悢無已，[一０]故殷勤示變，以悟陛下。

translations.literal: [9] Heaven’s care for Han never slackens; [10] therefore it shows portents again and again to wake Your Majesty.
translations.idiomatic: [9] Heaven still cares for Han [10] and presses omens on you to wake you up.

sentences.zh: 除妖去孽，實在修德。

translations.literal: To cast out prodigies and evil growth truly lies in cultivating virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Dispelling ill omens means restoring virtue.

sentences.zh: 臣位列台司，憂責深重，不敢尸祿惜生，坐觀成敗。

translations.literal: Your servant holds rank among the Three Dukes; the burden of care is heavy; I dare not draw salary in idleness, cling to life, and sit watching whether we thrive or fail.
translations.idiomatic: I stand among the Three Dukes—I cannot take pay and watch passively as the realm rises or falls.

sentences.zh: 如蒙采錄，使身首分裂，異門而出，所不恨也。

translations.literal: If you heed me, though my body be torn apart and head and feet leave by different gates, I will not resent it.
translations.idiomatic: If you accept this counsel, I will gladly die dismembered.

sentences.zh: 」[一一]帝諱其言切，托以蕃辟召非其人，遂策免之。

translations.literal: " [11] The emperor bridled at the bluntness; on the excuse that Fan’s recruitment had been wrong, he issued a rescript dismissing him.
translations.idiomatic: [11] The emperor hated the tone, invented a fault in Fan’s appointments, and dismissed him by edict.

sentences.zh: 注[一]關龍逢，桀臣。

translations.literal: Note [1] Guan Longfeng was a minister to Jie.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] Guan Longfeng served the tyrant Jie.

sentences.zh: 王子比干，紂諸父。

translations.literal: Prince Bigan was a paternal uncle of Zhou (of Shang).
translations.idiomatic: Bigan was King Zhou of Shang’s uncle.

sentences.zh: 二人並諫，悉皆誅死。

translations.literal: Both remonstrated; both were executed.
translations.idiomatic: Both spoke out; both were killed.

sentences.zh: 注[二]前書曰「君為元首，臣為股肱，明其一體相須而成」也。

translations.literal: Note [2] The earlier *Book of Han* says: "The ruler is the head; the ministers are the arms and thighs," showing that they are one body and need each other to succeed.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] The *Book of Han* calls ruler and minister head and limbs—one body.

sentences.zh: 注[三]秦始皇時，丞相李斯上言曰：「天下已定，百姓力農。

translations.literal: Note [3] In the time of the First Emperor, Chancellor Li Si presented a memorial, saying: "The realm is settled; the people labor in the fields.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3] Qin’s Li Si once wrote: “The empire is pacified; farmers toil in the fields.

sentences.zh: 今諸生好古，惑亂黔首，臣請史官非秦記及天下敢有藏詩、書、百家語者，悉燒之。

translations.literal: Now the scholars dote on antiquity and mislead the commoners; your servant requests that the historiographers’ records that are not Qin annals, and that under Heaven whoever dares hide the *Poems*, *Documents*, or the teachings of the hundred schools—all be burned."
translations.idiomatic: Scholar-officials peddle old texts and confuse the people—burn every non-Qin history and every copy of the classics and masters.”

sentences.zh: 」事見史記。

translations.literal: " The affair appears in the *Records of the Grand Historian*.
translations.idiomatic: The story is in the *Shiji*.

sentences.zh: □宏詔定古文官書序曰：「秦既焚書，患苦天下不從所改更，而諸生到者拜為郎，前後七百人。

translations.literal: Wei Hong’s preface to the collation of the ancient-text official documents says: "After Qin burned the books, fearing the realm would not follow its reforms, it summoned the scholars who came and made them Gentlemen—seven hundred in all before and after.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Hong’s preface says Qin, after the book burning, appointed seven hundred visiting scholars as Gentlemen but feared popular resistance.

sentences.zh: 乃密令種瓜於驪山坑谷中溫處，瓜實，詔博士說之，人人不同。

translations.literal: It secretly ordered melons planted in a warm gully on Mount Li; when the melons fruited, it ordered the erudites to explain the portent; every man gave a different answer.
translations.idiomatic: It grew melons in a warm Mount Li ravine, then asked the erudites to interpret the omen—each answer differed.

sentences.zh: 乃令就視，為伏機，諸生賢儒皆至焉，方相難不決，因發機從上填之以土，皆壓之，終乃無聲。

translations.literal: It then ordered them to go view; laying trip-traps, when the worthy ru had all come and were cross-examining one another without resolution, it sprang the mechanism and poured earth from above; all were crushed; in the end there was no sound.
translations.idiomatic: It lured the scholars in, sprang a trap, and buried them alive when they could not agree.

sentences.zh: 」今新豐縣溫湯處號愍儒鄉。

translations.literal: " Today the hot-spring site in Xinfeng County is called the Township of the Pitying of the Ru.
translations.idiomatic: That spot is now Xinfeng’s “Commiserating the Ru” township.

sentences.zh: 湯西有馬谷，西岸有坑，古老相傳以為秦坑儒處也。

translations.literal: West of the hot spring is Horse Valley; on the west bank is a pit; old tradition says it is where Qin buried the scholars.
translations.idiomatic: West lie Horse Valley and the pit said to hold Qin’s buried scholars.

sentences.zh: 注[四]史記武王克殷，命畢公表商容之閭，閎夭封比干之墓也。

translations.literal: Note [4] The *Records* says that when King Wu conquered Yin, he ordered the Duke of Bi to mark the lane of Shang Rong, and Hong Yao to seal Bi Gan’s tomb.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4] The *Shiji* says King Wu honored Shang Rong’s lane and sealed Bi Gan’s tomb.

sentences.zh: 注[五]詩小雅曰：「巧言如簧，顏之厚矣。

translations.literal: Note [5] The *Book of Songs*, "Minor Odes," says: "Glib words are like reed pipes; how thick is that cheek!"
translations.idiomatic: Note [5] A *Minor Ode* likens slander to a shrill reed pipe.

sentences.zh: 」簧，笙簧也。

translations.literal: " *Huang* means the reed pipe of a mouth organ.
translations.idiomatic: “Reed” here is the mouth-organ reed.

sentences.zh: 言讒人之口以喻笙簧也。

translations.literal: It likens the mouths of slanderers to reed pipes.
translations.idiomatic: The image compares slander to piping.

sentences.zh: 注[六]說菀曰：「禹見罪人，下車泣而問之。

translations.literal: Note [6] *Shuoyuan* says: "When Yu saw a criminal, he left his carriage, wept, and asked about him.
translations.idiomatic: Note [6] *Shuoyuan* says Yu wept over criminals.

sentences.zh: 左右曰：『夫罪人不順，故使殺焉，君王何為痛之至此也！

translations.literal: Those at his side said, "Criminals are disobedient, so they are made to be killed; why does my lord grieve to this extent!"
translations.idiomatic: Attendants said, “Criminals deserve death—why weep so hard?”

sentences.zh: 』禹曰：『堯舜之人，皆以堯舜之心為心。

translations.literal: " Yu said, "The people of Yao and Shun all took the hearts of Yao and Shun as their own heart.
translations.idiomatic: Yu answered, “Under Yao and Shun every heart matched the sage-kings.”

sentences.zh: 今寡人為君也，百姓各自以其心，是以痛之。

translations.literal: Now I, the little man, am ruler, yet the hundred surnames each follow his own heart—that is why I grieve."
translations.idiomatic: “As their ruler I see every man go his own way—that is why I weep.”

sentences.zh: 』」書曰：「百姓有罪，在予一人。

translations.literal: " The *Book* says: "If the hundred surnames have guilt, it lies in me alone."
translations.idiomatic: The *Book of Documents* adds, “The people’s sins are mine.”

sentences.zh: 」左傳曰：「禹湯罪己，其興也勃焉。

translations.literal: " The *Zuo Tradition* says: "Yu and Tang blamed themselves, and their rise was vigorous.
translations.idiomatic: The *Zuo* says shame brought Yu and Tang success.

sentences.zh: 桀紂罪人，其亡也忽焉。

translations.literal: Jie and Zhou blamed others, and their fall was sudden."
translations.idiomatic: Jie and Zhou blamed others and fell overnight.”

sentences.zh: 」杜預注曰：「勃，盛也。」

translations.literal: " Du Yu’s commentary says: "Vigorous means flourishing."
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu glosses “vigorous” as swift growth.

sentences.zh: 注[七]廣雅曰：「茹，食也。」

translations.literal: Note [7] The *Guangya* says: "Ru means to eat."
translations.idiomatic: Note [7] *Guangya* defines *ru* as “eat.”

sentences.zh: 注[八]論語孔子之言也。

translations.literal: Note [8] These are Confucius’s words in the *Analects*.
translations.idiomatic: Note [8] The line echoes Confucius in the *Analects*.

sentences.zh: 注[九]春秋感精符曰：「魯哀公政亂，絕無日食，天不譴告也。」

translations.literal: Note [9] *Chunqiu gan jing fu* says: "Duke Ai of Lu’s government was chaotic; there were no eclipses for days on end—Heaven issued no reproof."
translations.idiomatic: Note [9] An omen text says Heaven stopped warning wicked Lu.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]悢悢猶眷眷也。

translations.literal: Note [10] *Liang liang* is the same as *juan juan* (relentless care).
translations.idiomatic: Note [10] *Liang liang* means anxious, unceasing care.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]谷梁傳曰「公會齊侯於頰谷，齊人使擾施舞於魯之幕下。

translations.literal: Note [11] The *Guliang* says: "The duke met the marquis of Qi at Jia Valley; the men of Qi had the Rong of Rao perform a dance beneath Lu’s tent.
translations.idiomatic: Note [11] *Guliang* records the Jia Valley meeting where Qi humiliated Lu.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：『笑君者罪當死。

translations.literal: Confucius said, "Whoever mocks the ruler deserves death."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said anyone who mocked a ruler should die.

sentences.zh: 』使司馬行法焉，首足異門而出」也。

translations.literal: " He had the marshal apply the law; head and feet went out by different gates."
translations.idiomatic: The marshal executed the dancers—head and body out different gates.”

sentences.zh: [二]今帝祚未立，政事日蹙，諸君柰何委荼蓼之苦，息偃在默？

translations.literal: [2] Now the imperial succession is not yet settled; government grows daily more straitened—how can you gentlemen cast off the bitterness of *tu* and *liao*, and rest in silence?
translations.idiomatic: [2] No heir sits the throne; administration tightens—how can you idle in silence through such bitter days?

sentences.zh: [三]於義不足，焉得仁乎！

translations.literal: [3] As for duty it is not enough—how can there be benevolence!
translations.idiomatic: [3] To shirk duty is no humanity!”

sentences.zh: 」諸尚書惶怖，皆起視事。

translations.literal: " All the Masters of Writing were terrified and rose to attend their duties.
translations.idiomatic: The Ministers of Writing started in alarm and returned to work.

sentences.zh: 注[一]前書谷永曰「臣聞天生蒸人，不能相持，為立王者以統理之*

translations.literal: Note [1] The earlier *Book of Han* records Gu Yong saying: "Your servant has heard that Heaven begat the teeming people, who cannot sustain one another, so it established kings to govern them—
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] Gu Yong wrote that Heaven created people and set kings to rule them—

translations.literal: (for this reason)
translations.idiomatic: (hence)

translations.literal: " and so on.
translations.idiomatic: —and so forth.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]言人主雖亡，法度尚存，當行之與不亡時同，故曰「如存」。

translations.literal: Note [2] It means that although the ruler has perished, the laws and standards still exist; one should practice them as when he lived—hence "as if he were still here."
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] Even after a ruler dies, law survives—act as if he still ruled.

sentences.zh: 前書爰盎曰「主在與在，主亡與亡」也。

translations.literal: The earlier *Book of Han* records Yuan Ang saying, "While the lord lives I live with him; when the lord dies I die with him."
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Ang said he lived and died with his lord.

sentences.zh: 注[三]詩國風曰：「誰謂荼苦，其甘如薺。

translations.literal: Note [3] The *Airs of the States* says: "Who says *tu* is bitter? It is sweet as shepherd’s purse."
translations.idiomatic: Note [3] The *Airs* compare hardship to bitter herbs.

sentences.zh: 」周頌曰：「未堪家多難，予又集於蓼。」

translations.literal: " The *Odes of Zhou* say: "I could not bear the many troubles of the house; I have again gathered *liao*."
translations.idiomatic: The *Zhou Hymns* speak of gathering *liao* amid domestic disaster.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]內音納。

translations.literal: Note [2] *Nei* is read like *na* (to enter).
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] *Nei* is read *na*.

sentences.zh: 尚書曰「出納朕命」也。

translations.literal: The *Book of Documents* says "to bring in and out my commands."
translations.idiomatic: The *Book of Documents* uses *na* for transmitting orders.

sentences.zh: 注[三]齊宣王對閭丘滘曰：「夫士亦華發墮顛而後可用。

translations.literal: Note [3] King Xuan of Qi said to Lüqiu Kui: "A true gentleman’s hair may turn hoary and his crown fall back before he is fit for use."
translations.idiomatic: Note [3] King Xuan of Qi said a true minister only ripens with age.

sentences.zh: 」見新序。

translations.literal: " It is seen in *Xin xu*.
translations.idiomatic: The anecdote appears in *Xin xu*.

sentences.zh: 注[四]既，就也。

translations.literal: Note [4] *Ji* means "to approach, to take up."
translations.idiomatic: Note [4] *Ji* means to take up a post.

sentences.zh: 注[五]論語孔子曰：「富與貴是人之所欲，不以其道得之，不處也。」

translations.literal: Note [5] Confucius in the *Analects* says: "Wealth and honor are what people desire; if not gotten by the right Way, I will not dwell in them."
translations.idiomatic: Note [5] Confucius refuses wealth gained unjustly.

sentences.zh: 注[六]詩小雅曰「受爵不讓，至於已斯亡。

translations.literal: Note [6] A *Minor Ode* says "Receiving rank without yielding leads to ruin."
translations.idiomatic: Note [6] A *Minor Ode* warns that grabbing titles breeds ruin.

sentences.zh: 」注云：「爵祿不以相讓，故怨禍及之」也。

translations.literal: " The commentary says: "Because ranks and stipends were not yielded to one another, resentment and disaster reached them."
translations.idiomatic: The gloss ties ruin to grasping office without humility.

sentences.zh: 注[七]論語孔子曰：「及其老也，血氣既衰，戒之在得。

translations.literal: Note [7] Confucius in the *Analects* says: "In old age, when blood and qi have waned, beware greed."
translations.idiomatic: Note [7] Confucius warns old age against greed.

sentences.zh: 」注云：「得，貪也。」

translations.literal: " The commentary says: "De means greed."
translations.idiomatic: The gloss defines “gain” as greed.

sentences.zh: 初，桓帝欲立所幸田貴人為皇后。

translations.literal: At first Emperor Huan wished to establish the honored Lady Tian as empress.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Huan first meant to make his favorite Lady Tian empress.

sentences.zh: 蕃以田氏卑微，竇族良家，爭之甚固。

translations.literal: Fan argued that the Tian house was lowly while the Dou clan was a proper great family, and he disputed this very firmly.
translations.idiomatic: Fan insisted the Tians were too humble and the Dous a respectable house.

sentences.zh: 帝不得已，乃立竇後。

translations.literal: The emperor had no choice but to establish Empress Dou.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor yielded and raised Lady Dou.

sentences.zh: 及後臨朝，故委用於蕃。

translations.literal: When she later held court, she therefore entrusted authority to Fan.
translations.idiomatic: When she governed as regent, she leaned on Fan.

sentences.zh: 蕃與後父大將軍竇武，同心盡力，徵用名賢，共參政事，天下之士，莫不延頸想望太平。

translations.literal: Fan and her father, General-in-chief Dou Wu, worked with one heart, summoned famous worthies, and shared in governance; men of talent under Heaven craned their necks expecting peace.
translations.idiomatic: Fan and General Dou Wu filled office with talent; the realm hoped for peace.

sentences.zh: 而帝乳母趙嬈，旦夕在太后側，[一]中常侍曹節、王甫等與共交構，諂事太后。

translations.literal: But the emperor’s wet nurse Zhao Rao was morning and night at the empress dowager’s side; [1] Regular Palace Attendants Cao Jie, Wang Fu, and others colluded with her, fawning on the empress dowager.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Yet the wet nurse Zhao Rao haunted the empress dowager while eunuchs Cao Jie and Wang Fu flattered her.

sentences.zh: 太后信之，數出詔命，有所封拜，及其支類，多行貪虐。

translations.literal: The empress dowager trusted them; she repeatedly issued edicts and commands, enfeoffing and appointing; their kindred for the most part acted in greed and cruelty.
translations.idiomatic: She trusted them, showered offices on their kin, and let them run amok.

sentences.zh: 蕃常疾之，志誅中官，會竇武亦有謀。

translations.literal: Fan constantly hated this and intended to execute the inner attendants; it happened that Dou Wu also had a plan.
translations.idiomatic: Fan burned to purge the eunuchs; Dou Wu shared the plot.

sentences.zh: 蕃自以既從人望而德於太后，必謂其志可申，乃先上疏曰：「臣聞言不直而行不正，則為欺乎天而負乎人。

translations.literal: Fan considered that he had both popular esteem and obligation to the empress dowager, and was sure his aim could be carried out; he first presented a memorial, saying: "Your servant has heard that if one’s words are not straight and one’s conduct not upright, one deceives Heaven and betrays others.
translations.idiomatic: Fan thought his bond with the empress dowager guaranteed success and wrote: “Crooked words and crooked deeds cheat heaven and man.

sentences.zh: 危言極意，則腢凶側目，禍不旋踵。

translations.literal: To speak daring truth to the utmost turns the crowd of villains to glare with sidelong eyes; disaster comes without turning the heel.
translations.idiomatic: Blunt truth makes villains squint—disaster follows at once.

sentences.zh: 鈞此二者，臣寧得禍，不敢欺天也。

translations.literal: Weighing these two, your servant would rather meet disaster than deceive Heaven."
translations.idiomatic: I choose disaster over lying to heaven.”

sentences.zh: 今京師囂囂，道路諠嘩，言侯覽、曹節、公乘昕、王甫、鄭□等與趙夫人諸女尚書並亂天下。

translations.literal: Now the capital clamors; the roads are noisy with talk that Hou Lan, Cao Jie, Gongsheng Xin, Wang Fu, Zheng [ ], and the Lady Zhao’s women Masters of Writing together throw the realm into chaos.
translations.idiomatic: The capital buzzes that Hou Lan, Cao Jie, Gongsheng Xin, Wang Fu, Zheng [ ], and Dame Zhao’s female Masters of Writing ruin the state.

sentences.zh: [二]附從者升進，忤逆者中傷。

translations.literal: [2] Those who attach themselves are promoted; those who resist are slandered and struck down.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Flatterers rise; honest men are smeared.

sentences.zh: [三]方今一朝腢臣，如河中木耳，泛泛東西，耽祿畏害。
