translations.literal: [3] The courtiers of this age are like wood ear fungus in a river, drifting east and west, clinging to stipends and fearing harm.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Officials drift like kelp, chasing salary and safety.

sentences.zh: 陛下前始攝位，順天行誅，蘇康、管霸並伏其辜。

translations.literal: When Your Majesty first took the regency, you followed Heaven in executing punishment; Su Kang and Guan Ba both suffered for their crimes.
translations.idiomatic: At your accession you executed Su Kang and Guan Ba—

sentences.zh: 是時天地清明，人鬼歡喜，柰何數月復縱左右？

translations.literal: At that time Heaven and earth were clear; men and ghosts rejoiced—why after a few months again indulge those at your side?
translations.idiomatic: heaven and earth brightened—why after months coddle favorites again?

sentences.zh: 元惡大奸，莫此之甚。

translations.literal: No chief villain surpasses these.
translations.idiomatic: No villains worse walk the earth.

sentences.zh: 今不急誅，必生變亂，傾危社稷，其禍難量。

translations.literal: If you do not execute them swiftly, certain disorder will arise; the altars will be overturned; the harm cannot be measured.
translations.idiomatic: Delay will invite rebellion and imperil the altars.

sentences.zh: 願出臣章宣示左右，並令天下諸奸知臣疾之。

translations.literal: I wish you would publish your servant’s memorial to those at your side and let every villain in the realm know I abhor them.
translations.idiomatic: Publish this plea and show every schemer I oppose them.”

sentences.zh: 」太后不納，朝廷聞者莫不震恐。

translations.literal: " The empress dowager did not accept it; those at court who heard were all struck with fear.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager ignored it; the court trembled.

sentences.zh: 蕃因與竇武謀之，語在武傳。

translations.literal: Fan therefore consulted with Dou Wu; the language is in Wu’s biography.
translations.idiomatic: Fan then plotted with Dou Wu—the account is in Dou’s biography.

sentences.zh: 注[一]嬈音乃了反。

translations.literal: Note [1] *Rao* is read nai-liao fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] *Rao* is read *nai-liao*.

sentences.zh: 注[二]趙夫人即趙嬈也。

translations.literal: Note [2] "Lady Zhao" is Zhao Rao.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] “Lady Zhao” is the wet nurse Zhao Rao.

sentences.zh: 女尚書，宮內官也。

translations.literal: Women Masters of Writing are offices inside the palace.
translations.idiomatic: “Women Masters of Writing” are inner-palace posts.

sentences.zh: 注[三]前書劉向上書論王鳳曰「稱譽者登進，忤恨者誅傷」也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Liu Xiang’s memorial to Emperor Cheng on Wang Feng says: "Those who praise them rise; those who offend them are killed and wounded."
translations.idiomatic: Note [3] Liu Xiang said flatterers of the Wangs rose while critics died.

sentences.zh: 及事洩，曹節等矯詔誅武等。

translations.literal: When the affair leaked, Cao Jie and others forged an edict and executed Wu and the rest.
translations.idiomatic: When the plot leaked, Cao Jie forged an edict and killed Dou Wu and company.

sentences.zh: 蕃時年七十餘，聞難作，將官屬諸生八十餘人，並拔刃突入承明門，攘臂呼曰：「大將軍忠以衛國，黃門反逆，何雲竇氏不道邪？」

translations.literal: Fan was then more than seventy; hearing that disaster had broken out, he led more than eighty subordinates and students, all drawing blades and bursting in through the Chenming Gate, baring their arms and shouting: "The general-in-chief was loyal in defending the state; the Yellow Gates rebel—how can you say the Dou clan was without the Way?"
translations.idiomatic: Fan, past seventy, led eighty armed followers through Chenming Gate shouting Dou Wu had been loyal and the eunuchs traitors.

sentences.zh: 王甫時出，與蕃相迕，[一]適聞其言，而讓蕃曰：「先帝新□天下，山陵未成，竇武何功，兄弟父子，一門三侯？

translations.literal: Wang Fu happened to be coming out and crossed Fan; [1] hearing these words, he rebuked Fan: "The late emperor had newly [ ] the realm; the mausoleum was not yet finished—what merit had Dou Wu, that brothers and father and sons—three marquises in one gate?
translations.idiomatic: Wang Fu met him [1] and sneered: “The late emperor had barely settled the realm—what merited three Dou marquises?

sentences.zh: 又多取掖庭宮人，作樂飲燕，旬月之閒，貲財億計。

translations.literal: He moreover took many palace women from the lateral courts, made music and feasted for weeks on end, amassing cash by the hundred million within a month or so.
translations.idiomatic: He filled his halls with palace women and burned fortunes on revelry.”

sentences.zh: 大臣若此，是為道邪？

translations.literal: If great ministers act thus, is that the Way?
translations.idiomatic: Is that how ministers behave?”

sentences.zh: 公為棟樑，枉橈阿黨，復焉求賊！

translations.literal: You are the pillar and beam; you bend crookedly and party with them—where else will you seek the traitors!
translations.idiomatic: You prop the state yet abet them—where are the real rebels?”

sentences.zh: 」遂令收蕃。

translations.literal: " He then ordered Fan seized.
translations.idiomatic: He had Fan arrested.

sentences.zh: 蕃拔□叱甫，甫兵不敢近，乃益人圍之數十重，遂執蕃送黃門北寺獄。

translations.literal: Fan drew his blade and shouted at Fu; Fu’s troops dared not close in, so they brought in more men and surrounded him layer upon layer, then seized Fan and delivered him to the Northern Prison of the Yellow Gates.
translations.idiomatic: Fan brandished a sword and cursed Wang Fu until the guards hung back; reinforcements ringed him in ranks, then dragged him to the eunuchs’ northern jail.

sentences.zh: 黃門從官騶[二]蹋踧蕃曰：「死老魅！

translations.literal: Yellow Gate attendants and mounted escorts [2] trampled and kicked Fan, saying, "Dead old demon!
translations.idiomatic: Eunuch runners [2] kicked Fan and jeered, “You decrepit fiend—

sentences.zh: 復能損我曹員數，奪我曹稟假不？

translations.literal: Can you still cut our numbers and strip our stipends and advances?"
translations.idiomatic: can you still slash our ranks and cancel our pay?”

sentences.zh: 」即日害之。

translations.literal: " That same day they murdered him.
translations.idiomatic: They killed him that day.

sentences.zh: 徙其家屬於比景，宗族、門生、故吏皆斥免禁錮。

translations.literal: His family dependents were banished to Bijng; clansmen, disciples, and former subordinates were all cashiered and banned from office.
translations.idiomatic: His kin went to Bijng; his lineage, students, and old staff lost their posts under proscription.

sentences.zh: 注[一]迕猶遇也。

translations.literal: Note [1] *Wu* means to meet, to encounter.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] *Wu* here means “meet.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]騶，騎士也。

translations.literal: Note [2] *Zou* means mounted escort.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] *Zou* denotes outriders.

sentences.zh: 蕃友人陳留朱震，時為銍令，[一]聞而□官哭之，收葬蕃屍，匿其子逸於甘陵界中。

translations.literal: Fan’s friend Zhu Zhen of Chenliu was at that time magistrate of Zhi; [1] hearing the news he left office and mourned, gathered Fan’s corpse for burial, and hid Fan’s son Yi within the borders of Ganling.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Zhen of Chenliu, magistrate of Zhi, [1] quit his post to mourn Fan, buried him, and hid his son Chen Yi in Ganling.

sentences.zh: 事覺系獄，合門桎梏。

translations.literal: When the matter came to light he was imprisoned; the whole household wore fetters.
translations.idiomatic: Discovery landed him in chains with his entire household.

sentences.zh: 震受考掠，誓死不言，故逸得免。

translations.literal: Zhen suffered interrogation under torture but swore to die rather than speak, so Yi escaped.
translations.idiomatic: He endured the rack in silence and saved Yi.

sentences.zh: 後黃巾賊起，大赦黨人，乃追還逸，官至魯相。

translations.literal: Later, when the Yellow Turban rebels rose, a general amnesty restored the faction lists, and Yi was recalled; he rose to chancellor of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: When the turbans forced a broad pardon for the “faction,” Yi returned and eventually became chancellor of Lu.

sentences.zh: 注[一]銍，縣，屬沛郡。

translations.literal: Note [1] *Zhi* is a county in Pei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] Zhi county lies in Pei commandery.

sentences.zh: 震字伯厚，初為州從事，奏濟陰太守單匡臧罪，並連匡兄中常侍車騎將軍超。

translations.literal: Zhen’s courtesy name was Bohou; he had first served as a commandery clerk, memorializing the Jiyin governor Shan Kuang for embezzlement, implicating Kuang’s elder brother, the Regular Palace Attendant and General of Chariots and Cavalry Chao.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Zhen, style Bohou, had indicted Jiyin’s governor Shan Kuang and snared Kuang’s brother, the eunuch general Chao.

sentences.zh: 桓帝收匡下廷尉，以譴超，超詣獄謝。

translations.literal: Emperor Huan arrested Kuang and sent him to the Commandant of Justice to reproach Chao, and Chao went to the prison to apologize.
translations.idiomatic: Huan caged Kuang to pressure Chao, who humbly presented himself at the jail.

sentences.zh: 三府諺曰：「車如雞棲馬如狗，疾惡如風朱伯厚。」

translations.literal: A proverb in the three high offices ran: "Carriages like chicken roosts, horses like dogs—hating evil like a gale, Zhu Bohou."
translations.idiomatic: Ministers joked that carriages looked like chicken coops yet Zhu “hated evil like a gale.”

sentences.zh: 論曰：桓、靈之世，若陳蕃之徒，鹹能樹立風聲，抗論惛俗。

translations.literal: The summation says: In the ages of Huan and Ling, men such as Chen Fan could establish their reputation and debate against a benighted age.
translations.idiomatic: The historian reflects: under Huan and Ling, men like Chen Fan built reputations and defied a darkened world.

sentences.zh: 而驅馳嶮□之中，與刑人腐夫同朝爭衡，[一]終取滅亡之禍者，彼非不能絜情志，違埃霧也。

translations.literal: Yet they galloped through precipitous peril, contending on level terms at court with mutilated men and corrupt eunuchs; [1] in the end they met the disaster of annihilation—not because they could not have cleansed their intent and shunned the mists.
translations.idiomatic: They still jousted in treacherous straits beside castrates—not for want of moral clarity.

sentences.zh: [二] 愍夫世士以離俗為高，而人倫莫相恤也。

translations.literal: [2] Alas for scholars of the age who made withdrawal the mark of nobility, while human relations went unheeded.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Yet their age prized reclusion and ignored human duty.

sentences.zh: 以遯世為非義，故屢退而不去；

translations.literal: They took flight from the world as unrighteous, so though they often retired they would not stay gone;
translations.idiomatic: Retreat felt immoral, so they kept returning to office;

sentences.zh: 以仁心為己任，雖道遠而彌厲。

translations.literal: they took a humane heart as their trust, and though the Way was distant they pressed ever harder.
translations.idiomatic: they shouldered benevolence though the path grew steeper.

sentences.zh: [三]及遭際會，協策竇武，自謂萬世一遇也。

translations.literal: [3] When their moment came, they joined Dou Wu in counsel, believing it a meeting once in ten thousand ages.
translations.idiomatic: [3] When fate paired them with Dou Wu, they thought heaven had offered a once-in-an-age chance.

sentences.zh: 懍懍乎伊、望之業矣！

translations.literal: Awe-inspiring—like the achievements of Yi Yin and Lü Wang!
translations.idiomatic: They aimed as high as Yi Yin and Lü Wang.

sentences.zh: [四]功雖不終，然其信義足以攜持民心。

translations.literal: [4] Though their work did not reach completion, their good faith was enough to carry the people’s hearts.
translations.idiomatic: [4] They failed to finish, yet their integrity still won the people.

sentences.zh: 漢世亂而不亡，百餘年閒，數公之力也。

translations.literal: Han’s age was chaotic yet did not perish; for more than a hundred years that was the strength of such men.
translations.idiomatic: Han survived a century of chaos partly through men like them.

sentences.zh: 注[一]前書班固曰：「相與提衡。

translations.literal: Note [1] Ban Gu in the earlier *Book of Han* says: "They raised the balance together."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] Ban Gu says rivals “raised the balance together.”

sentences.zh: 」音義云：「衡，平也。

translations.literal: The gloss on sounds and meanings says: "Balance means level.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss explains *balance* as level scales—

sentences.zh: 言二人齊也。」

translations.literal: It means the two men were equal."
translations.idiomatic: meaning evenly matched opponents.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]違，避也。

translations.literal: Note [2] *Wei* means to avoid.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] *Wei* means to shun.

sentences.zh: 注[三]論語曰：「仁以為己任，不亦重乎！

translations.literal: Note [3] The *Analects* says: "To take benevolence as one’s charge—is that not weighty!
translations.idiomatic: Note [3] Confucius asked whether bearing benevolence is not the heaviest burden—

sentences.zh: 死而後已，不亦遠乎！」

translations.literal: To die only then to stop—is that not far!"
translations.idiomatic: and whether stopping only at death is not the longest road.”

sentences.zh: 注[四]懍懍，有風采之貌也。

translations.literal: Note [4] *Lin lin* is the manner of one with striking presence.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4] *Lin lin* describes a commanding demeanor.

sentences.zh: 王允字子師，太原祁人也。

translations.literal: Wang Yun, courtesy name Zishi, was a native of Qi in Taiyuan.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Yun, style Zishi, came from Qi in Taiyuan.

sentences.zh: [一]世仕州郡為冠蓋。

translations.literal: [1] For generations his family had served in commandery and province as leading notables.
translations.idiomatic: [1] His forebears had long held prominent local office.

sentences.zh: 同郡郭林宗嘗見允而奇之，曰：「王生一日千里，王佐才也。

translations.literal: Guo Tai of the same commandery once saw Yun and marveled, saying, "This young man advances a thousand *li* in a day; he has the stuff of a kingly assistant."
translations.idiomatic: Guo Tai exclaimed that Wang advanced “a thousand *li* a day” and had the makings of a chief minister.

sentences.zh: 」[二]遂與定交。

translations.literal: " [2] They thereupon formed a lasting tie.
translations.idiomatic: He and Guo became sworn friends.

sentences.zh: 注[一]祁，今并州縣也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Qi is a county, now a county in Bing Province.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] Qi is today a county in Shanxi.

sentences.zh: 注[二]史記曰，田光謂燕太子丹曰：「臣聞驥壯盛之時，一日千里；

translations.literal: Note [2] The *Records* says Tian Guang told Crown Prince Dan of Yan: "I have heard that a fine steed in its prime may cover a thousand *li* in a day;
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] Prince Dan’s retainer compared a steed’s peak years to a thousand-*li* day;

sentences.zh: 至其老也，駑馬先之。」

translations.literal: in old age a nag outruns it."
translations.idiomatic: in age even a hack beats it.”

sentences.zh: 年十九，為郡吏。

translations.literal: At nineteen he became a commandery clerk.
translations.idiomatic: At nineteen he served as a commandery clerk.

sentences.zh: 時小黃門晉陽趙津貪橫放恣，為一縣巨患，允討捕殺之。

translations.literal: At that time the Junior Yellow Gate Zhao Jin of Jinyang was greedy, violent, and unrestrained—the great scourge of a county; Yun pursued, captured, and executed him.
translations.idiomatic: He hunted down the predatory eunuch’s client Zhao Jin of Jinyang and executed him.

sentences.zh: 而津兄弟諂事宦官，因緣譖訴，桓帝震怒，征太守劉□，遂下獄死。

translations.literal: But Jin’s brothers fawned on the inner attendants and through connections slandered [Yun]; Emperor Huan was furious, summoned Governor Liu [ ], and sent him to prison to die.
translations.idiomatic: Jin’s kin bribed eunuchs; Huan summoned Governor Liu [ ] and had him die in jail.

sentences.zh: 允送喪還平原，終畢三年，然後歸家。

translations.literal: Yun escorted the funeral to Pingyuan, completed the full three years, and then returned home.
translations.idiomatic: Wang escorted Liu’s bier to Pingyuan, observed three years’ mourning, then went home.

sentences.zh: 復還仕，郡人有路佛者，少無名行，而太守王球召以補吏，允犯顏固爭，球怒，收允欲殺之。

translations.literal: He returned to office; there was a fellow townsman named Lu Fo who in youth had no reputation or conduct, yet Administrator Wang Qiu summoned him to fill a clerkship; Yun offended Qiu’s dignity and argued stubbornly; Qiu grew angry, seized Yun, and intended to kill him.
translations.idiomatic: Back in service he defied Administrator Wang Qiu’s appointment of the worthless Lu Fo; Qiu jailed him for execution.

sentences.zh: 刺史鄧盛聞而馳傳闢為別駕從事。

translations.literal: Inspector Deng Sheng heard of it and raced with relay credentials to appoint him Attendant Clerk for the Separate Carriage.
translations.idiomatic: Inspector Deng Sheng whisked him out as Attendant Clerk for the Separate Carriage.

sentences.zh: 允由是知名，而路佛以之廢□。

translations.literal: From this Yun became famous, while Lu Fo on his account was dismissed [ ].
translations.idiomatic: Wang’s fame dates from that spat; Lu Fo lost his post.

sentences.zh: 注[二]傳，逮也。

translations.literal: Note [2] *Chuan* means to summon, to apprehend.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] *Chuan* means to summon for arrest.

sentences.zh: 會赦，還復刺史。

translations.literal: It happened that there was an amnesty; he returned and again became inspector.
translations.idiomatic: An amnesty restored him as inspector.

sentences.zh: 旬日閒，復以它罪被捕。

translations.literal: Within ten days or so he was again arrested on another charge.
translations.idiomatic: Within days another charge jailed him.

sentences.zh: 司徒楊賜以允素高，不欲使更楚辱，[一]乃遣客謝之曰：「君以張讓之事，故一月再征。

translations.literal: Minister of Education Yang Ci, because Yun had always been lofty in conduct, did not wish to see him suffer further torture and humiliation; [1] he therefore sent a client to apologize, saying, "On account of the affair with Zhang Rang you have been summoned twice within a month.
translations.idiomatic: Minister Yang Ci, honoring Wang’s character, sent a messenger: “Zhang Rang’s grudge has you summoned twice in a month—

sentences.zh: 凶慝難量，幸為深計。

translations.literal: The viciousness is hard to fathom; I hope you will take deep counsel."
translations.idiomatic: think hard how deep the malice runs.”

sentences.zh: 」[二] 又諸從事好氣決者，共流涕奉藥而進之。

translations.literal: " [2] Moreover the clerks who loved decisive action together wept and offered him poison to drink.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Hot-blooded colleagues wept and brought poison.

sentences.zh: 允厲聲曰：「吾為人臣，獲罪於君，當伏大辟以謝天下，豈有乳藥求死乎！

translations.literal: Yun said in a stern voice: "I am a subject of the ruler; having offended the ruler, I ought to submit to the capital punishment to apologize to all under heaven—how could I drink milk-poison seeking death!"
translations.idiomatic: Wang thundered, “A minister answers for his faults with the axe—he does not take poison to cheat the law.”

sentences.zh: 」投杯而起，出就檻車。

translations.literal: " He cast aside the cup, rose, and went out to the caged cart.
translations.idiomatic: He threw down the cup and entered the prison wagon.

sentences.zh: 既至廷尉，左右皆促其事，朝臣莫不歎息。

translations.literal: When he reached the Commandant of Justice, those on both sides hurried the case along; no courtier failed to sigh.
translations.idiomatic: At the Commandant’s yamen officials pressed for a verdict; the whole court groaned.

sentences.zh: 大將軍何進、太尉袁隗、司徒楊賜共上疏請之曰：「夫內視反聽，則忠臣竭誠；

translations.literal: General-in-chief He Jin, Grand Commandant Yuan Wei, and Minister of Education Yang Ci jointly presented a memorial asking for him, saying: "When one looks inward and listens inward, loyal ministers give their utmost;
translations.idiomatic: He Jin, Yuan Wei, and Yang Ci memorialized: “Introspection moves loyal ministers—

sentences.zh: 寬賢矜能，則義士厲節。

translations.literal: when one is lenient to the worthy and cherishes ability, men of duty steel their integrity.
translations.idiomatic: clemency toward talent steels honest men.”

sentences.zh: [三]是以孝文納馮唐之說，[四] 晉悼宥魏絳之罪。

translations.literal: [3] Hence Emperor Wen accepted Feng Tang’s argument; [4] Duke Dao of Jin pardoned Wei Jiang’s offense.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Emperor Wen heard Feng Tang; [4] Jin Dao forgave Wei Jiang.”

sentences.zh: [五]允以特選受命，誅逆撫順，曾未期月，州境澄清。

translations.literal: [5] Yun was specially chosen and commissioned to punish the rebellious and soothe the compliant; not yet a full month had passed when the province was cleared.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Wang, specially appointed to pacify the province, cleared it inside a month—

sentences.zh: 方欲列其庸勳，請加爵賞，而以奉事不當，當肆大戮。

translations.literal: We were about to list his merit and request additional noble reward, yet for handling affairs improperly he is liable to the extreme punishment.
translations.idiomatic: yet you would execute him for a procedural slip.

sentences.zh: 責輕罰重，有虧觿望。

translations.literal: The fault is light but the penalty heavy—it disappoints all expectation.
translations.idiomatic: The crime is slight and the sentence cruel—it breaks faith with the realm.

sentences.zh: 臣等備位宰相，不敢寢默。

translations.literal: We, occupying the places of chancellors, dare not sleep in silence.
translations.idiomatic: We ministers cannot stay mute.

sentences.zh: 誠以允宜蒙三槐之聽，以昭忠貞之心。

translations.literal: We sincerely believe Yun ought to receive the hearing at the Three *Huai*—to display loyal constancy.
translations.idiomatic: He deserves the three-acacia audience that signals fair judgment of loyalty.”

sentences.zh: 」[六]書奏，得以減死論。

translations.literal: " [6] When the memorial was presented, he was allowed a reduced sentence short of death.
translations.idiomatic: [6] The memorial commuted his sentence.

sentences.zh: 是冬大赦，而允獨不在宥，三公鹹復為言。

translations.literal: That winter there was a great amnesty, yet Yun alone was not within mercy; the three dukes all spoke for him again.
translations.idiomatic: Winter’s general amnesty skipped Wang until the three dukes intervened.

sentences.zh: 至明年，乃得解釋。

translations.literal: Only the next year was he released.
translations.idiomatic: He walked free the following year.

sentences.zh: 是時宦者橫暴，睚□觸死。

translations.literal: At that time the eunuchs were violent and oppressive; a sidelong glance could mean death.
translations.idiomatic: Eunuchs then killed for a dirty look.

sentences.zh: [七]允懼不免，乃變易名姓，轉側河內、陳留閒。

translations.literal: [7] Yun feared he could not escape; he changed his name and wandered between Henei and Chenliu.
translations.idiomatic: [7] Fearing arrest, Wang Yun went underground between Henei and Chenliu.

sentences.zh: [九] 注[一]更，經也。

translations.literal: [9] Note [1] *Geng* means to pass through, to experience.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] *Geng* means to undergo.

sentences.zh: 楚，苦痛。

translations.literal: *Chu* means painful suffering.
translations.idiomatic: *Chu* means torment.

sentences.zh: 注[二]深計謂令自死。

translations.literal: Note [2] "Deep counsel" means instructing him to kill himself.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] “Deep counsel” here means suicide.

sentences.zh: 注[三]內視，自視也。

translations.literal: Note [3] *Nei shi* means to look at oneself.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3] *Nei shi* is introspection.

sentences.zh: 反聽，自聽也。

translations.literal: *Fan ting* means to listen to oneself.
translations.idiomatic: *Fan ting* is listening inward.

sentences.zh: 言皆恕己，不責於人也。

translations.literal: It means they all forgive themselves and do not blame others.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss urges forgiving oneself before judging others.

sentences.zh: 注[四]文帝時，魏尚為雲中守，下吏免。

translations.literal: Note [4] In Emperor Wen’s time Wei Shang was governor of Yunzhong; inferior officials dismissed him.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4] Wendi cashiered Wei Shang of Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 馮唐為郎中署長，奏言曰：「臣聞魏尚為雲中守，上功首虜差六級，陛下下之吏，削其爵。

translations.literal: Feng Tang, chief of the palace gentlemen section, presented a memorial saying: "Your servant has heard that Wei Shang as governor of Yunzhong reported enemy heads six ranks short; Your Majesty sent him to the officials and stripped his rank.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Tang reminded the throne that Wei Shang’s head count fell six short—Wendi demoted him—

sentences.zh: 愚以為陛下法太明，賞太輕，罰太重。

translations.literal: Your foolish servant thinks Your Majesty’s laws are too bright, rewards too light, penalties too heavy."
translations.idiomatic: and pleaded that the law was harsh and rewards stingy.”

sentences.zh: 」帝即日赦尚復為雲中太守。

translations.literal: " That same day the emperor pardoned Shang and restored him as governor of Yunzhong.
translations.idiomatic: Wendi reinstated Wei Shang the same day.

sentences.zh: 注[五]左傳曰，晉悼公之弟楊干亂行於曲梁，魏絳戮其僕。

translations.literal: Note [5] The *Zuo Tradition* says: Duke Dao of Jin’s younger brother Yang Gan threw the march into disorder at Quliang; Wei Jiang executed his driver.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5] Wei Jiang executed Yang Gan’s driver at Quliang.

sentences.zh: 公怒之。

translations.literal: The duke was angry with him.
translations.idiomatic: The duke raged.

sentences.zh: 絳曰：「臣聞師觿以順為武，軍事有死無犯為敬。

translations.literal: Jiang said: "Your servant has heard that for an army to follow orders is martial prowess; in military affairs respect lies in dying without violating discipline.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Jiang said armies honor obedience—death before disorder—

sentences.zh: 臣懼其死，以及楊干，無所逃罪。

translations.literal: Your servant feared execution; it reached Yang Gan; there was no escaping guilt.
translations.idiomatic: and he could not escape guilt for striking the duke’s kin.”

sentences.zh: 」公曰：「寡人有弟不能教訓，使干大命，寡人之過也。

translations.literal: " The duke said: "I have a younger brother I could not instruct, causing Gan to offend the great command—it is my fault.
translations.idiomatic: The duke answered, “My brother’s crime is my fault—

sentences.zh: 子無重寡人之過。

translations.literal: Do not, sir, double my fault."
translations.idiomatic: do not double my shame.”

sentences.zh: 」與之禮食，使佐新軍。

translations.literal: " He treated him with ritual feasting and made him assistant commander of the new army.
translations.idiomatic: He feasted Wei Jiang and promoted him.

sentences.zh: 注[六]周禮朝士職，三槐、九□，公卿於下聽訟，故曰「三槐之聽」。

translations.literal: Note [6] The office of court gentlemen in the *Rites of Zhou*, at the three *huai* and nine *ji*, had dukes and ministers hear lawsuits below—hence "hearing at the three *huai*."
translations.idiomatic: Note [6] Zhou ritual placed judges beneath the court’s three acacia trees—hence “three-acacia hearing.”

sentences.zh: 注[七]睚音五懈反。

translations.literal: Note [7] *Ya* is read wu-jie fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [7] *Ya* is read *wu-jie*.

sentences.zh: □音士懈反。

translations.literal: [ ] is read shi-jie fan.
translations.idiomatic: The next graph is read *shi-jie*.

sentences.zh: 前書曰：「原涉好殺，睚□於塵中，觸死者甚多。」

translations.literal: The earlier *Book of Han* says: "Yuan She loved killing; a sidelong *ya* in the dust—those who died from contact were very many."
translations.idiomatic: The *Book of Han* says Yuan She killed over trifling slights.

sentences.zh: 注[八]轉側猶去來也。

translations.literal: Note [8] *Zhuan ce* means coming and going.
translations.idiomatic: Note [8] *Zhuan ce* means drifting about.

sentences.zh: 及帝崩，乃奔喪京師。

translations.literal: When the emperor died, he hurried to the capital for mourning.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor died Wang rushed to Luoyang to mourn.

sentences.zh: 時大將軍何進欲誅宦官，召允與謀事，請為從事中郎，轉河南尹。

translations.literal: At that time General-in-chief He Jin wished to execute the eunuchs; he summoned Yun to consult, appointed him Consultant to the General of the Household, then transferred him to Governor of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: He Jin, plotting against the eunuchs, made Wang a staff adviser and then Henan governor.

sentences.zh: 獻帝即位，拜太僕，再遷守尚書令。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xian ascended, [Yun] was appointed Minister of the Household, then transferred twice to acting Prefect of the Masters of Writing.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xian named him Minister of the Household and then acting Prefect of the Masters of Writing.

sentences.zh: 初平元年，代楊彪為司徒，守尚書令如故。

translations.literal: In the first year of Chuping he replaced Yang Biao as Minister of Education while continuing as acting Prefect of the Masters of Writing.
translations.idiomatic: Chuping 1 made him Minister of Education while he kept the prefecture.

sentences.zh: 及董卓遷都關中，允悉收斂蘭台、石室圖書秘緯要者以從。

translations.literal: When Dong Zhuo moved the capital into the Guanzhong pass, Yun collected everything essential from the Orchid Terrace and Stone Chamber archives—charts, books, and secret weft texts—and brought them along.
translations.idiomatic: When Dong Zhuo shifted the capital west, Wang packed the palace archives and esoteric texts.

sentences.zh: 既至長安，皆分別條上。

translations.literal: Once they reached Chang’an, he catalogued and submitted each category.
translations.idiomatic: In Chang’an he filed inventories for each collection.

sentences.zh: 又集漢朝舊事所當施用者，一皆奏之。

translations.literal: He also assembled precedents from Han rule that ought still to be applied, and memorialized on every item.
translations.idiomatic: He also codified usable Han precedents and submitted them piecemeal.

sentences.zh: 經籍具存，允有力焉。

translations.literal: The classics survived intact—Yun had great part in it.
translations.idiomatic: That the canon survived owes much to Wang Yun.

sentences.zh: 時董卓尚留洛陽，朝政大小，悉委之於允。

translations.literal: At that time Dong Zhuo still remained in Luoyang; court affairs great and small were all entrusted to Yun.
translations.idiomatic: With Dong Zhuo left in Luoyang, Wang ran the court.

sentences.zh: 允矯情屈意，每相承附，卓亦推心，不生乖疑，故得扶持王室於危亂之中，臣主內外，莫不倚恃焉。

translations.literal: Yun feigned compliance and bent his will, always attaching himself when occasion offered; Zhuo in turn gave his heart and did not breed suspicion—thus he was able to shore up the royal house amid peril and chaos; ruler and ministers, inside and out, all leaned on him.
translations.idiomatic: Wang played the loyal confidant until Dong trusted him and let him steady the throne amid turmoil—court and palace leaned on his façade.

sentences.zh: 允見卓禍毒方深，篡逆已兆，密與司隸校尉黃琬、尚書鄭公業等謀共誅之。

translations.literal: Yun saw that Zhuo’s bane ran deep and the portents of usurpation had already appeared; he secretly plotted with Metropolitan Superintendent Huang Wan, Master of Writing Zheng Ye, and others jointly to kill him.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing usurpation ahead, Wang Yun secretly allied with Huang Wan and Zheng Ye to kill Dong Zhuo.

sentences.zh: 乃上護羌校尉楊瓚行左將軍事，執金吾士孫瑞為南陽太守，並將兵出武關道，以討袁術為名，實欲分路征卓，而後拔天子還洛陽。

translations.literal: He therefore promoted Colonel Protecting the Qiang Yang Zan to conduct affairs as General of the Left, Bearer of the Mace Sun Rui to governor of Nanyang, both commanding troops out by the Wu Pass road—under pretext of campaigning against Yuan Shu but intending actually to divide forces against Zhuo and afterward rescue the Son of Heaven back to Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: He staged Yang Zan as Left-General designate and Sun Rui as Nanyang governor with troops through Wu Pass—ostensibly against Yuan Shu but really to pinch Dong Zhuo and bring the emperor home.

sentences.zh: 卓疑而留之，允乃引內瑞為僕射，瓚為尚書。

translations.literal: Zhuo grew suspicious and held them back; Yun thereupon brought Rui in as Vice Director of the Imperial Secretariat and Zan in as a Master of Writing.
translations.idiomatic: Dong grew wary and kept them in Chang’an, so Wang installed Rui and Zan in the secretariat instead.

sentences.zh: 二年，卓還長安，錄入關之功，封允為溫侯，食邑五千戶。

translations.literal: In the second year Zhuo returned to Chang’an, credited those who had entered the pass, and enfeoffed Yun as marquis of Wen with five thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: In year 2 Dong Zhuo rewarded the Guanzhong contingent and made Wang Yun marquis of Wen at five thousand households.

sentences.zh: 固讓不受。

translations.literal: [Yun] firmly declined and would not accept.
translations.idiomatic: Wang refused the fief.

sentences.zh: 士孫瑞說允曰：「夫執謙守約，存乎其時。

translations.literal: Sun Rui urged Yun, saying: "To hold modesty and keep to restraint depends on the times.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Rui argued that modesty must suit the moment—

sentences.zh: 公與董太師並位俱封，而獨崇高節，豈和光之道邪？

translations.literal: You share rank and enfeoffment with Grand Tutor Dong yet alone exalt high integrity—is that the way of blending your radiance with the dust?
translations.idiomatic: sharing honors with Dong while playing the hermit is not Laozi's 'blend your radiance with the dust.'

sentences.zh: 」[一]允納其言，乃受二千戶。

translations.literal: " [1] Yun accepted his words and took two thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Wang yielded and accepted two thousand households.

sentences.zh: 注[一]老子曰：「和其光，同其塵。」

translations.literal: Note [1] *Laozi* says: "Blend your radiance; be one with the dust."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] *Laozi* counsels merging one’s gleam with the mud of the world.

sentences.zh: 三年春，連雨六十餘日，允與士孫瑞、楊瓚登台請霽，復結前謀。

translations.literal: In the third year spring it rained continuously for more than sixty days; Yun mounted a terrace with Sun Rui and Yang Zan to pray for clearing skies and renewed the earlier plot.
translations.idiomatic: Spring of year 3 brought sixty days of rain; Wang, Sun Rui, and Yang Zan climbed a terrace to pray for clear weather and revived the assassination plan.

sentences.zh: [一]瑞曰：「自歲末以來，太陽不照，霖雨積時，月犯執法，[二]彗孛仍見，晝陰夜陽，霧氣交侵，此期應促盡，內發者勝。

translations.literal: [1] Rui said: "From year’s end the sun has not shone; soaking rains piled up for seasons; the moon trespassed on *zhifa*; [2] comets appeared again and again; daylight was overcast while nights were bright; fog and vapor crossed and invaded—the time portends haste to the limit; an inner blow will win.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Sun Rui cited omens—overcast days, bright nights, comets, moon on *Zhifa*—and said inside help must strike soon.

sentences.zh: 幾不可後，公其圖之。

translations.literal: The opportunity can hardly be delayed any further; you must plan it.
translations.idiomatic: “Act now—do not wait.”

sentences.zh: 」允然其言，乃潛結卓將呂布，使為內應。

translations.literal: " Yun assented; he thereupon secretly won over Zhuo’s general Lü Bu to work from within.
translations.idiomatic: Wang agreed and recruited Lü Bu for an inside job.

sentences.zh: 會卓入賀，呂布因刺殺之。

translations.literal: It happened that Zhuo came in to offer New Year felicitations; Lü Bu thereupon stabbed him to death.
translations.idiomatic: When Dong Zhuo came to wish the court a new year, Lü Bu ran him through.

sentences.zh: 語在卓傳。

translations.literal: The account is in Zhuo’s biography.
translations.idiomatic: Details fill Dong Zhuo’s biography.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]說文曰：「霽，雨止也。

translations.literal: [3] Note [1] The *Shuowen* says: "Ji means the rain has stopped."
translations.idiomatic: [3] Note [1] *Shuowen* defines *ji* (clearing) as rain’s end.

sentences.zh: 」郭璞曰：「南陽人呼雨止曰霽。」

translations.literal: " Guo Pu says: "In Nanyang they call a break in the rain *ji*."
translations.idiomatic: Nanyang folk call a break in rain *ji*.

sentences.zh: 注[二]執法，星名。

translations.literal: Note [2] *Zhifa* is the name of a star.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] *Zhifa* is an asterism.

sentences.zh: 史記曰「太微南四星曰執法」也。

translations.literal: The *Records* says: "The four stars south of the Supreme Tenuity are *Zhifa*."
translations.idiomatic: The *Shiji* places *Zhifa* among the four stars south of the Purple Palace.

sentences.zh: 注[三]帝時疾愈，故入賀也。

translations.literal: Note [3] The emperor’s illness had improved, so [Zhuo] came in to offer congratulations.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3] Dong entered court because the emperor had recovered.

sentences.zh: 允初議赦卓部曲，呂布亦數勸之。

translations.literal: Yun at first proposed amnesty for Zhuo’s troops; Lü Bu also several times urged it.
translations.idiomatic: Wang first wanted to spare Dong’s soldiers; Lü Bu agreed.

sentences.zh: 既而疑曰：「此輩無罪，從其主耳。

translations.literal: Afterward he hesitated, saying: "Those men are not guilty; they only followed their lord.
translations.idiomatic: Then Wang wavered: “Those troops only obeyed their lord—

sentences.zh: 今若名為惡逆而特赦之，適足使其自疑，非所以安之之道也。

translations.literal: Now if we label them wicked rebels yet specially pardon them, that only suffices to make them suspect themselves—it is not the way to reassure them."
translations.idiomatic: pardoning them while branding their master may only spook them.”

sentences.zh: 」呂布又欲以卓財物班賜公卿、將校，允又不從。

translations.literal: " Lü Bu also wished to distribute Zhuo’s goods to the high officials and generals; Yun again would not agree.
translations.idiomatic: Lü wanted to share Dong’s hoard with ministers; Wang refused.

sentences.zh: 而素輕布，以□客遇之。

translations.literal: And he had always held Bu in light esteem, treating him as a [ ] retainer.
translations.idiomatic: He still treated Lü as a mere horseman.

sentences.zh: 布亦負其功勞，多自誇伐，既失意望，漸不相平。

translations.literal: Bu in turn relied on his achievements, often boasting; having failed in his expectations, he gradually felt unequal treatment.
translations.idiomatic: Lü, proud of the kill, bragged and sulked when denied spoils.

sentences.zh: 允性剛稜疾惡，[一]初懼董卓豺狼，故折節圖之。

translations.literal: Yun by nature was harsh and keen to hate evil; [1] at first he feared Zhuo the wolf, so he bent his will to work against him.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Wang was rigid and intolerant—he had only flattered Dong out of fear.

sentences.zh: 卓既殲滅，自謂無復患難，及在際會，每乏溫潤之色，杖正持重，不循權宜之計，是以腢下不甚附之。

translations.literal: Once Zhuo was destroyed, he thought there was no further peril; but when the moment of rule came, he always lacked a genial mien, bore the staff of rectitude and held to weighty principle, would not follow expedient plans—so those below did not much cleave to him.
translations.idiomatic: After Dong died he dropped the mask—stern, legalistic, no room for compromise—so he lost the camp.

sentences.zh: 注[一]稜，威稜也，力登反。

translations.literal: Note [1] *Leng* means awe-inspiring edge; the reading is *li-deng* fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] *Leng* means sharp severity (*li-deng* fan).

sentences.zh: 董卓將校及在位者多涼州人，允議罷其軍。

translations.literal: Among Zhuo’s generals and serving officials many were men of Liang Province; Yun proposed disbanding their army.
translations.idiomatic: Many officers were Liang men; Wang aimed to strip their troops.

sentences.zh: 或說允曰：「涼州人素憚袁氏而畏關東。

translations.literal: Someone urged Yun: "The Liang Province men have always feared the Yuan house and stood in awe of the region east of the pass.
translations.idiomatic: An adviser warned that Liang soldiers feared the Yuans and the Guandong hosts—

sentences.zh: 今若一旦解兵*

translations.literal: If now in one morning you disarm them*
translations.idiomatic: if you disarm them in one stroke—

sentences.zh: (關東)

translations.literal: (east of the pass)
translations.idiomatic: (re: Guandong)

sentences.zh: *，則必人人自危。

translations.literal: * they are sure to feel danger to every man.
translations.idiomatic: —they will panic.

sentences.zh: 可以皇甫義真為將軍，就領其觿，因使留陝以安撫之，而徐與關東通謀，以觀其變。

translations.literal: You can make Huangfu Yizhen general, place him in command of their host, then station him in Shan to pacify them, and slowly join counsel with the region east of the pass to watch how things change."
translations.idiomatic: Better put Huangfu Song in charge, garrison Shan, calm the west, and watch Guandong.”

sentences.zh: 」允曰：「不然。

translations.literal: " Yun said: "Not so.
translations.idiomatic: Wang said no—

sentences.zh: 關東舉義兵者，皆吾徒耳。

translations.literal: The men who raised righteous troops east of the pass are all our kind."
translations.idiomatic: “The Guandong allies are on our side.”

sentences.zh: 今若距險屯陝，雖安涼州，而疑關東之心，甚不可也。

translations.literal: Now if you hold the passes and camp at Shan, though you pacify Liang Province you will greatly strengthen suspicion in the east—that is not acceptable."
translations.idiomatic: Fortifying Shan would spook the allies he trusted.

sentences.zh: 」時百姓訛言，當悉誅涼州人，遂轉相恐動。

translations.literal: " At that time the common people circulated a rumor that they were about to execute every Liang Province man; fear spread from person to person.
translations.idiomatic: Rumors swore Wang would massacre Liang men.

sentences.zh: 其在關中者，皆擁兵自守。

translations.literal: Those in the Guanzhong pass all grasped arms and guarded themselves.
translations.idiomatic: Guanzhong garrisons armed themselves.

sentences.zh: 更相謂曰：「丁彥思、蔡伯喈但以董公親厚，並尚從坐。

translations.literal: They said to one another: "Ding Yansi and Cai Bosai were kin and intimate with Lord Dong only, yet they still suffered collective punishment.
translations.idiomatic: They whispered that Ding and Cai Yong, though only Dong’s friends, had been massacred—

sentences.zh: 今既不赦我曹，而欲解兵，今日解兵，明日當復為魚肉矣。

translations.literal: Now if they will not pardon us but wish to disband the army—today we disband the army; tomorrow we shall again be fish and meat."
translations.idiomatic: “If Wang disarms us we will be butcher’s meat.”

sentences.zh: 」卓部曲將李傕、郭汜等先將兵在關東，因不自安，遂合謀為亂，攻圍長安。

translations.literal: " Zhuo’s commanders Li Jue and Guo Si had first led troops east of the pass; they therefore felt insecure, plotted together to rebel, attacked, and besieged Chang’an.
translations.idiomatic: Li Jue and Guo Si, camped in Guandong, revolted and besieged Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 城陷，呂布奔走。

translations.literal: When the city fell, Lü Bu fled.
translations.idiomatic: Chang’an fell; Lü Bu bolted.

sentences.zh: 布駐馬青瑣門外，[一]招允曰：「公可以去乎？

translations.literal: Bu halted his horse outside the Cyan-Green Lattice Gate, [1] calling to Yun: "Sir, can you not leave?"
translations.idiomatic: Outside the green-lattice gate Lü asked Wang to ride with him.

sentences.zh: 」允曰：「若蒙社稷之靈，上安國家，吾之願也。

translations.literal: " Yun said: "If by the numinous favor of the altars of soil and grain we secure the state above, that is my wish;
translations.idiomatic: Wang said saving the boy emperor was his duty—

sentences.zh: 如其不獲，則奉身以死之。

translations.literal: if that is not gained, then I will offer my person to die for it.
translations.idiomatic: if not he would die with the court.

sentences.zh: 朝廷幼少，恃我而已，[二]臨難苟免，吾不忍也。

translations.literal: The court is young and relies on me alone; [2] to face peril and seek bare escape—I cannot bear it.
translations.idiomatic: [2] He could not abandon the child emperor.

sentences.zh: 努力謝關東諸公，勤以國家為念。

translations.literal: Strive to convey my thanks to the lords east of the pass; diligently keep the state in mind."
translations.idiomatic: He begged Lü to urge Guandong to remember the throne.”

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]前書音義曰：「以青畫戶邊鏤中，天子制也。

translations.literal: " Note [1] The earlier *Book of Han* glossary says: "Painting the lattice in cyan within the door-screen—this is the Son of Heaven’s regulation."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] The Han commentary describes the cyan-green lattice as imperial.

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]朝廷謂天子也。

translations.literal: " Note [1] "The court" means the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] “Court” here means the emperor.

sentences.zh: 初，允以同郡宋翼為左馮翊，王宏為右扶風。

translations.literal: At first Yun had appointed Song Yi of the same commandery as Governor of Left Fengyi and Wang Hong as Governor of Right Fufeng.
translations.idiomatic: Wang had given Song Yi Zuo Fengyi and Wang Hong You Fufeng.

sentences.zh: 是時三輔民庶熾盛，兵谷富實，李傕等欲即殺允，懼二郡為患，乃先征翼、宏。

translations.literal: At that time the three *fu* teemed with people; arms and grain were rich. Li Jue and the rest wished to kill Yun at once but feared trouble from the two commanderies, so they first summoned Yi and Hong.
translations.idiomatic: The Sanfu was populous and rich; the rebels feared Song and Wang’s bases, so they summoned the two governors first.

sentences.zh: 宏遣使謂翼曰：「郭汜、李傕以我二人在外，故未危王公。

translations.literal: Hong sent a messenger to tell Yi: "Guo Si and Li Jue have not yet harmed the honored Wang because the two of us are outside.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Hong warned Song Yi: “Li and Guo spare Wang only while we hold the east—

sentences.zh: 今日就征，明日俱族。

translations.literal: If today we answer the summons, tomorrow we will all be exterminated as clans.
translations.idiomatic: obey the summons and we die tomorrow.”

sentences.zh: 計將安出？

translations.literal: What plan can there be?"
translations.idiomatic: What is your plan?”

sentences.zh: 」翼曰：「雖禍福難量，然王命所不得避也。

translations.literal: " Yi said: "Though fortune and misfortune are hard to measure, the royal command cannot be evaded."
translations.idiomatic: Song answered that one cannot flee an imperial summons.

sentences.zh: 」宏曰：「義兵鼎沸，在於董卓，況其黨與乎！

translations.literal: " Hong said: "Righteous hosts boil like a cauldron on account of Dong Zhuo—how much more his partisans!
translations.idiomatic: Wang Hong cried that war still raged against Dong’s clique—

sentences.zh: 若舉兵共討君側惡人，山東必應之，此轉福為福之計也。

translations.literal: If we raise arms together to punish the wicked men at the ruler’s side, the lands east of the mountains are sure to respond—this is the plan to turn misfortune into fortune."
translations.idiomatic: rebel, rally Shanxi, and turn disaster into hope.”

sentences.zh: 」翼不從。

translations.literal: " Yi would not follow.
translations.idiomatic: Song refused.

sentences.zh: 宏不能獨立，遂俱就征，下廷尉。

translations.literal: Hong could not act alone, so they both answered the summons and were sent down to the Commandant of Justice.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Hong could not move alone; both went to prison.

sentences.zh: 傕乃收允及翼、宏，並殺之。

translations.literal: Jue thereupon seized Yun together with Yi and Hong and killed them all.
translations.idiomatic: Li Jue then executed Wang, Song, and Wang Hong.

sentences.zh: 允時年五十六。

translations.literal: Yun was then fifty-six years old.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Yun was fifty-six.

sentences.zh: 長子侍中蓋、次子景、定及宗族十餘人皆見誅害，唯兄子晨、陵得脫歸鄉里。

translations.literal: His eldest son, Palace Attendant Gai, his second sons Jing and Ding, and more than ten clansmen were all killed; only his nephews Chen and Ling escaped and returned to their home commandery.
translations.idiomatic: His sons Gai, Jing, Ding, and a dozen kin died; nephews Chen and Ling fled home.

sentences.zh: 天子感慟，百姓喪氣，莫敢收允屍者，唯故吏平陵令趙戩□官營喪。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven was moved and grieved; the common people lost heart; none dared collect Yun’s corpse—only his former subordinate, the magistrate of Pingling Zhao Jian, left office and arranged the funeral.
translations.idiomatic: None dared bury Wang except Zhao Jian, who quit his post as Pingling magistrate to bury him.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]戩音翦。

translations.literal: [1] Note [1] *Jian* is read *jian* with the *jian* initial.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Note [1] *Jian* is read *jian* (翦).

sentences.zh: 王宏字長文，少有氣力，不拘細行。

translations.literal: Wang Hong, courtesy name Changwen, in youth had physical strength and did not fuss over small conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Hong, style Changwen, was strong and rough in his youth.

sentences.zh: 初為弘農太守，考案郡中有事宦官買爵位者，雖位至二千石，皆掠考收捕，遂殺數十人，威動鄰界。

translations.literal: He first became governor of Hongnong, investigating the commandery for those who had bought official rank through the eunuchs; even if their rank reached two thousand *dan*, he had them flogged, arrested, and in the end killed several dozen—his might shook neighboring regions.
translations.idiomatic: As Hongnong governor he flogged and executed dozens of men who had bought office from eunuchs.

sentences.zh: 素與司隸校尉胡種有隙，及宏下獄，種遂迫促殺之。

translations.literal: He had long been at odds with Metropolitan Superintendent Hu Zhong; when Hong went down to prison, Zhong thereupon pressed the case and had him killed.
translations.idiomatic: He feuded with Hu Zhong, who rushed his execution in jail.

sentences.zh: 宏臨命詬[一]曰：「宋翼豎儒，不足議大計。

translations.literal: As Hong faced death he scolded, [1] saying: "Song Yi is a pedantic coward, unfit to discuss great plans.
translations.idiomatic: Dying, he cursed: “Song Yi is a weak pedant—

sentences.zh: [二]胡種樂人之禍，禍將及之。

translations.literal: [2] Hu Zhong delights in others’ disasters; disaster will reach him."
translations.idiomatic: [2] Hu Zhong who gloats at doom will meet his own.”

sentences.zh: 」種後眠輒見宏以杖擊之，因發病，數日死。

translations.literal: " Afterward whenever Zhong slept he saw Hong striking him with a staff; he then fell ill and in a few days died.
translations.idiomatic: Hu Zhong thereafter dreamed of Wang Hong beating him and died within days.

sentences.zh: 注[一]詬，罵也，音火豆反。

translations.literal: Note [1] *Gou* means to curse; the reading is *huo-dou* fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] *Gou* means revile (*huo-dou* fan).

sentences.zh: 注[二]豎者，言賤劣如僮豎。

translations.literal: Note [2] *Shu* means base and mean like a child slave.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] *Shu* insults Song as servile.

sentences.zh: 後遷都於許，帝思允忠節，使改殯葬之，遣虎賁中郎將奉策弔祭，賜東園秘器，贈以本官印綬，送還本郡。

translations.literal: Later the capital moved to Xu; the emperor remembered Yun’s loyalty and had his burial changed; he sent a colonel of the Tiger Loyal Guard with edicts to offer condolences and sacrifice, granted an inner-park coffin, presented the seals and ribbons of his original office, and sent him back to his native commandery.
translations.idiomatic: After the move to Xu the court reburied Wang with imperial honors and restored his regalia.

sentences.zh: 封其孫黑為安樂亭侯，食邑三百戶。

translations.literal: He enfeoffed his grandson Hei as marquis of Anle hamlet with three hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: His grandson Wang Hei received three hundred households at Anle.

sentences.zh: 士孫瑞

translations.literal: Sun Rui
translations.idiomatic: Sun Rui (section heading)

sentences.zh: 士孫瑞字君策，扶風人，頗有才謀。

translations.literal: Sun Rui, courtesy name Junce, was a man of Fufeng, quite talented in strategy.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Rui of Fufeng, style Junce, was a clever strategist.

sentences.zh: 瑞以允自專討董卓之勞，故歸功不侯，所以獲免於難。

translations.literal: Because Rui shifted credit for planning the execution of Zhuo entirely to Yun, he declined enfeoffment and therefore escaped the catastrophe.
translations.idiomatic: He credited Wang Yun alone for killing Dong and refused a fief, which saved his skin.

sentences.zh: 後為國三老、光祿大夫。

translations.literal: Later he became one of the Three Elders of the state and Minister of the Imperial Clan.
translations.idiomatic: He later served as state elder and Minister of the Imperial Clan.

sentences.zh: 每三公缺，楊彪、皇甫嵩皆讓位於瑞。

translations.literal: Whenever one of the three dukes was vacant, Yang Biao and Huangfu Song both yielded the place to Rui.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Biao and Huangfu Song repeatedly tried to yield grand offices to him.

sentences.zh: 興平二年，從駕東歸，為亂兵所殺。

translations.literal: In the second year of Xingping he followed the imperial train eastward and was killed by mutinous troops.
translations.idiomatic: In Xingping 2 he escorted the emperor east and died to mutineers.

sentences.zh: 趙戩字叔茂，長陵人，性質正多謀。

translations.literal: Zhao Jian, courtesy name Shumao, was a man of Changling, upright by nature and full of plans.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Jian of Changling, style Shumao, was principled and resourceful.

sentences.zh: 初平中，為尚書，典選舉。

translations.literal: In the Chuping era he was a Master of Writing in charge of nominations.
translations.idiomatic: During Chuping he oversaw appointments as a Master of Writing.

sentences.zh: 董卓數欲有所私授，戩輒堅拒不聽，言色強厲。

translations.literal: Dong Zhuo several times wished to make private appointments; Jian flatly refused and would not listen, his words and expression stern.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo repeatedly demanded favors; Zhao refused flatly.

sentences.zh: 卓怒，召將殺之，觿人悚慄，而戩辭貌自若。

translations.literal: Zhuo grew angry and summoned him intending to kill him; the whole hall trembled in fear, yet Jian’s bearing remained as before.
translations.idiomatic: Dong called for his head; the hall shook while Zhao stayed calm.

sentences.zh: 卓悔，謝釋之。

translations.literal: Zhuo repented, apologized, and released him.
translations.idiomatic: Dong thought better of it and let him go.

sentences.zh: 長安之亂，容於荊州，劉表厚禮焉。

translations.literal: During the Chang’an turmoil he took refuge in Jing Province; Liu Biao treated him with generous ritual.
translations.idiomatic: When Chang’an fell he fled to Liu Biao.

sentences.zh: 及曹操平荊州，乃辟之，執戩手曰：「恨相見晚。

translations.literal: When Cao Cao pacified Jing Province, he summoned him, grasped Jian’s hand, and said, "I regret we met so late."
translations.idiomatic: Cao Cao seized Jingzhou and greeted Zhao with “late to meet you.”

sentences.zh: 」卒相國鐘繇長史。

translations.literal: " He ended as senior clerk to Chancellor Zhong Yao.
translations.idiomatic: He finished as senior clerk to Zhong Yao the chancellor.
Note marker [1].

sentences.zh: 注[一]鐘繇字符常，魏太祖時為相國。

translations.literal: Note [1] Zhong Yao, courtesy name Yuanchang, was chancellor under Wei Emperor Taizu.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] Zhong Yao (Yuanchang) served Cao Cao as chancellor.

sentences.zh: 論曰：士雖以正立，亦以謀濟。

translations.literal: The summation says: Though a gentleman stands by rectitude, he is also helped by strategy.
translations.idiomatic: The historian concludes: virtue needs strategy.

sentences.zh: 若王允之推董卓而引其權，伺其閒而敝其罪，當此之時，天子懸解矣。

translations.literal: In Wang Yun’s case—deferring to Zhuo to draw his power, watching for an opening to expose his crimes—in that moment the Son of Heaven hung freed.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Yun flattered Dong until he could strike—freeing the throne for a moment.

sentences.zh: [一]而終不以猜忤為釁者，知者本於忠義之誠也。

translations.literal: [1] That he never took suspicion and friction as reason for a rupture shows that the wise rest ultimately on loyal righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Yet he never broke faith because his heart stayed loyal.

sentences.zh: 故推卓不為失正，分權不為苟冒，伺閒不為狙詐。

translations.literal: Hence pushing Zhuo was not losing rectitude, dividing power was not reckless presumption, watching for an opening was not deceit.
translations.idiomatic: Flattery, patience, and stratagem served integrity in the end.

sentences.zh: 及其謀濟意從，則歸成於正也。

translations.literal: When their plan succeeded and intent followed through, the achievement returned to rectitude.
translations.idiomatic: Success crowned his moral aim.

sentences.zh: 注[一]莊子曰：「斯所謂帝之懸解。

translations.literal: Note [1] *Zhuangzi* says: "This is what is called the Emperor’s hanging release."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] *Zhuangzi* calls such relief the emperor’s “hanging release.”

sentences.zh: 」懸解喻安泰也。

translations.literal: " *Xuan jie* illustrates peace and security.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss explains *xuan jie* as ease.

translations.literal: 【Appraisal heading】
translations.idiomatic: Section heading: Appraisal.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：陳蕃蕪室，志清天綱。

translations.literal: The encomium says: Chen Fan’s overgrown chamber—his aim was to cleanse heaven’s net.
translations.idiomatic: The verse praises Chen Fan’s weed-choked yard and his wish to mend heaven’s net—

sentences.zh: 人謀雖緝，幽運未當。

translations.literal: Though human plans were in accord, the hidden workings of fate were not yet right.
translations.idiomatic: Men’s plans aligned, but dark fate was not on their side.

sentences.zh: [一]言觀殄瘁，曷非雲亡？

translations.literal: [1] We see the state wither—was it not because the worthy "passed like clouds?"
translations.idiomatic: [1] The state withered when good men vanished like clouds.

sentences.zh: [二]子師圖難，晦心傾節。

translations.literal: [2] Zishi schemed through peril, veiling his heart and bowing his integrity.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Wang Yun hid his mind and bent his principles to unseat the tyrant.

sentences.zh: [三]功全元丑，身殘餘孽。

translations.literal: [3] The deed undid the arch-villain; his body paid the price to leftover evil.
translations.idiomatic: [3] He slew the monster yet died to the dregs of that evil.

sentences.zh: 時有隆夷，事亦工拙。

translations.literal: The times have their rise and fall; success or failure also turns on skill or clumsiness.
translations.idiomatic: Fortune shifts; outcomes turn on deft or bungled policy.
[4] (endnote)

sentences.zh: 注[一]緝，合也。

translations.literal: Note [1] *Ji* means to join, to agree.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] *Ji* means “unite.”

sentences.zh: 易下系曰：「人謀鬼謀。

translations.literal: The *Book of Changes*, lower *Xici*, says: "Human counsel, ghostly counsel."
translations.idiomatic: The *Zhou yi* speaks of human and spirit counsel.

sentences.zh: 」言蕃設謀雖合，而冥運未符也。

translations.literal: " It means though Fan’s plans were sound, the hidden turn of fate did not match.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss: Chen Fan’s plans fit, but fate did not.

sentences.zh: 注[二]殄，盡也。

translations.literal: Note [2] *Tian* means to be exhausted, used up.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] *Tian* means “exhaust.”

sentences.zh: 瘁，病也。

translations.literal: *Cui* means sick, afflicted.
translations.idiomatic: *Cui* means “wither.”

sentences.zh: 言國將殄瘁，豈不由賢人云亡乎？

translations.literal: It means the state is on the verge of *tian cui*—how can it not be because the worthy "passed like clouds?"
translations.idiomatic: The state sickens to death when the worthy vanish.

sentences.zh: 詩大雅曰「人之雲亡，邦國殄瘁」也。

translations.literal: The *Book of Songs*, "Great Odes," says: "When good men pass like clouds, the state is *tian cui*."
translations.idiomatic: The *Great Ode* line blames the state’s collapse on the loss of good men.

sentences.zh: 注[三]謂矯性屈意於董卓。

translations.literal: Note [3] It means he bent his nature and submitted his will to Dong Zhuo.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3] That glosses Wang Yun’s feigned servility to Dong Zhuo.

sentences.zh: 注[四]誅卓為工，被殺為拙也。

translations.literal: Note [4] Executing Zhuo was skill; being killed was clumsiness.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4] Killing Dong was the deft move; dying to the sequel was the blunder.

sentences.zh: 校勘記

translations.literal: Collation notes
translations.idiomatic: Section heading: textual collation notes.

sentences.zh: 二一六０頁七行埏隧今人墓道也按：汲本「人」作「入」。

translations.literal: Page 2160 line 7 *yan sui* “what people today call the tomb passage”—Annotation: the Ji edition writes “enter” [入] for “person” [人].
translations.idiomatic: Critical note: Ji text reads 入 for 人 in the *yan sui* gloss.

sentences.zh: 二一六０頁一一行稍遷拜尚書按：校補謂案文「拜」上當有「召」字。

translations.literal: Page 2160 line 11 "Gradually promoted, appointed Master of Writing"—Annotation: textual supplements argue the text should have "summoned" [召] before "appointed" [拜].
translations.idiomatic: Scholars add 召 before 拜 for “summoned and appointed.”

sentences.zh: 二一六一頁六行當伏*[重]*誅據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 2161 line 6 "ought to suffer *[severe]* punishment"—Supplied from Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Lacuna filled from Ji and Palace eds.

sentences.zh: 二一六二頁一行而令天下之論按：刊誤謂案文「令」當作「今」。

translations.literal: Page 2162 line 1 "And *ling* the realm’s discourse"—Error publication argues *ling* should be *jin* (now).
translations.idiomatic: Emend 令 → 今 per *Kanwu*.

sentences.zh: 二一六二頁九行車駕幸廣*

translations.literal: Page 2162 line 1 "the imperial train visited Guang*"
translations.idiomatic: Line break for Guang—

translations.literal: (cheng)
translations.idiomatic: (cheng)

sentences.zh: **[成]*校獵按：集解引錢大昕說，謂「城」當作「成」，馬融上廣成頌，即此。

translations.literal: "**[cheng]* for a camp hunt"—Annotation: Qian Daxin argues *cheng* [城] should be *cheng* [成]; Ma Rong’s "Ode on Guangcheng" refers to this.
translations.idiomatic: Qian Daxin: read Guangcheng; Ma Rong’s rhapsody refers to it.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Adopted in this edition.

sentences.zh: 注同。

translations.literal: Same in the commentary.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary aligned.

sentences.zh: 二一六二頁一０行無教逸游按：「教」原斗「放」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2162 line 10 "Do not teach dissolute wandering"—Annotation: "teach" [教] was corrupted to "release" [放]; corrected from Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: 教 corrected from 放 per Ji/Palace.

sentences.zh: 二一六二頁一二行有三空之□哉按：校補引柳從辰說，謂御覽四五二引本書，「□」作「危」。

translations.literal: Page 2162 line 12 "when there is peril among the three voids"—Annotation: Liu Congchen cites *Yulan* 452 quoting this text with "danger" [危] for the lacuna.
translations.idiomatic: Lacuna read as 危 per *Yulan*.

sentences.zh: 二一六二頁一三行又*

translations.literal: Page 2162 line 13 "Moreover*"
translations.idiomatic: Moreover—

translations.literal: (earlier)
translations.idiomatic: (earlier)

sentences.zh: *秋*[前]*多雨據殿本改。

translations.literal: "*early autumn* had much rain"—Emended per Palace edition.
translations.idiomatic: “Early autumn” reading from Palace ed.

sentences.zh: 二一六三頁一０行文武兼姿按：刊誤謂姿是姿貌，此當作「資」。

translations.literal: Page 2163 line 10 "combining civil and military *zi*"—Annotation: *Kanwu* says *zi* is bearing; here it should be *zi* [資] "endowment."
translations.idiomatic: *姿 → 資 (talent) per *Kanwu*.

sentences.zh: 二一六三頁一五行時小黃門趙津按：錢大昕謂據王允傳稱「小黃門晉陽趙津」，此傳「小黃門」下無「晉陽」字，則「二郡」文不可通矣。

translations.literal: Page 2163 line 15 "At that time the Junior Yellow Gate Zhao Jin"—Annotation: Qian Daxin notes Wang Yun’s biography says "Junior Yellow Gate Zhao Jin of Jinyang"; without "Jinyang" here the "two commanderies" line fails.
translations.idiomatic: Qian Daxin: add Jinyang from Wang Yun’s biography.

sentences.zh: 二一六三頁一五行南陽大猾張*

translations.literal: Page 2163 line 15 "the great scoundrel of Nanyang Zhang*"
translations.idiomatic: Nanyang’s Zhang—

translations.literal: (si)
translations.idiomatic: (si)

sentences.zh: **[泛]*據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: "**[fan]*—Emended per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: —Fan—per Ji/Palace.

sentences.zh: 下同。

translations.literal: Same below.
translations.idiomatic: Same below.

sentences.zh: 按：岑晊傳作「張泛」，泛與泛同。

translations.literal: Annotation: Cen Zhi’s biography writes "Zhang Fan"; *fan* is the same as *fan*.
translations.idiomatic: Cf. Cen Zhi: 张泛 = 张泛.

sentences.zh: 二一六四頁一行而並竟考殺之按：刊誤謂案漢、魏鞠獄皆云「考竟」，此誤倒。

translations.literal: Page 2164 line 1 "yet in the end interrogated and killed them"—Annotation: *Kanwu* says Han and Wei trial records say *kao jing*; here the graphs are reversed.
translations.idiomatic: *考竟* order corrected per *Kanwu*.

sentences.zh: 二一六四頁三行蕃與司徒劉矩集解引惠棟說，謂考異雲時胡廣為司徒，非矩也，棟案劉愷傳，考異非也。

translations.literal: Page 2164 line 3 "Fan together with Minister of Education Liu Ju"—Jijie cites Hui Dong: textual variants say Hu Guang was Minister then, not Liu Ju; Hui follows Liu Kai’s biography—variants are wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong vs. Hu Guang as minister—see Liu Kai bio note.

sentences.zh: 今按：劉矩未嘗為司徒，考異說是。

translations.literal: Editorial: Liu Ju never served as Minister; the variant view is correct.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ju was never Minister; *Kaoyi* right.

sentences.zh: 劉愷傳亦誤，參閱劉愷傳校記。

translations.literal: Liu Kai’s biography is also wrong—see its collation note.
translations.idiomatic: See Liu Kai collation.

sentences.zh: 二一六四頁一一行營惑聖聽按：何焯校本改「營」為「熒」。

translations.literal: Page 2164 line 11 "encircle and delude the sage hearing"—Annotation: He Chuo’s collated edition changes *ying* to *ying* [熒] "dazzle."
translations.idiomatic: *營 → 熒 per He Chuo.

sentences.zh: 二一六五頁四行長*

translations.literal: Page 2165 line 4 "long*"
translations.idiomatic: long—

translations.literal: (li)
translations.idiomatic: (li)

sentences.zh: **[史]*已下多至抵罪刊誤謂案文「吏」當作「史」，太尉府有長史，故因蕃見譴也。

translations.literal: "**[shi]* clerks and below"—*Kanwu*: *li* [吏] should be *shi* [史]; the Grand Commandant’s office has a chief clerk, hence Fan’s reproof.
translations.idiomatic: 長史 not 長吏 per *Kanwu*.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Adopted.

sentences.zh: 二一六五頁五行□字幼平陝人按：「陝」原斗「陜」，逕據汲本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2165 line 5 "[ ] courtesy Youping, man of Shan"—Annotation: "Shan" [陝] was miswritten "narrow pass" [陜]; corrected from Ji edition.
translations.idiomatic: *陜 → 陝 per Ji ed.

sentences.zh: 二一六七頁一一行說菀曰汲本、殿本「菀」作「苑」。

translations.literal: Page 2167 line 11 "*Shuoyuan* says"—Ji and Palace editions write *yuan* [苑] for *wan* [菀].
translations.idiomatic: *菀*/*苑* variant in *Shuoyuan* title.

sentences.zh: 按：苑菀通。

translations.literal: Annotation: *yuan* and *wan* are interchangeable.
translations.idiomatic: The two graphs interchange.

sentences.zh: 二一六八頁七行不能相持殿本「持」作「治」。

translations.literal: Page 2168 line 7 "cannot sustain one another"—Palace edition "sustain" [持] reads "govern" [治].
translations.idiomatic: 殿本 持 vs 治.

sentences.zh: 按：「治」作「持」，避唐諱改。

translations.literal: Annotation: "govern" was altered to "sustain" to avoid the Tang taboo.
translations.idiomatic: *治* → *持* for Tang taboo.

sentences.zh: 二一六八頁七行為立王者以統理之*

translations.literal: Page 2168 line 7 "so kings were set up to govern them*"
translations.idiomatic: kings to rule them—

translations.literal: (for this reason)
translations.idiomatic: The gloss reads “therefore” (gu).

sentences.zh: *也據殿本刪，與前書谷永傳合。

translations.literal: "* etc."—Deleted per Palace edition to match the earlier *Book of Han* Gu Yong biography.
translations.idiomatic: The particle gu was deleted per the Palace edition to match Gu Yong’s biography in the *Book of Han*.

sentences.zh: 二一六八頁八行法度尚存按：汲本、殿本「存」作「在」。

translations.literal: Page 2168 line 8 "laws and standards still exist"—Annotation: Ji and Palace editions write "are" [在] for "exist" [存].
translations.idiomatic: Editions disagree on whether to write *cun* or *zai* for “remain.”

sentences.zh: 二一六八頁一五行使皇天震怒按：「震」原斗「振」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2168 line 15 "made heavenly majesty thereby rage"—Annotation: "shake" [振] was corrupted to "quake" [震]; corrected from Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: The graph *zhen* (shake) was miswritten as *zhen* (thunderclap) and is restored from the Ji and Palace recensions.

sentences.zh: 二一七一頁七行並連匡兄中常侍車騎將軍超按：校補謂宦者傳又謂匡為超弟之子。

translations.literal: Page 2171 line 7 "and implicated Kuang’s elder brother the Regular Palace Attendant"—Annotation: textual supplements note the eunuch biography also calls Kuang Chao’s nephew.
translations.idiomatic: The eunuch treatise makes Shan Kuang a nephew of Chao instead of an elder brother.

sentences.zh: 二一七一頁一一行而人倫莫相恤也按：李慈銘謂治要「莫」下有「能」字，當據增。

translations.literal: Page 2171 line 11 "while human relations went unheeded"—Annotation: Li Ciming says *Zhiyao* has "could not" [能] after "not" [莫]; should be added.
translations.idiomatic: 增“能” per *Zhiyao* / Li Ciming.

sentences.zh: 二一七一頁一一行及遭際會按：李慈銘謂治要「遭」下有「值」字，當據增。

translations.literal: Page 2171 line 11 "when their moment came"—Annotation: Li Ciming says *Zhiyao* has "met" [值] after "encountered" [遭]; should be added.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ciming argues that the *Zhiyao* recension adds the graph for "encounter" after "suffer" and that the main text should be emended to match that witness.

sentences.zh: 二一七二頁一０行復還仕郡人有路佛者按：張森楷謂「郡」下當更有一「郡」字。

translations.literal: Page 2172 line 10 "returned to office; there was a fellow townsman Lu Fo"—Annotation: Zhang Senkai says there should be another "commandery" [郡] after "commandery."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Senkai suggests inserting a second occurrence of the word commandery in that clause so that the syntax reads clearly.

sentences.zh: 二一七二頁一三行上除禁黨按：李慈銘謂「禁黨」當作「黨禁」。

translations.literal: Page 2172 line 13 "removed the faction ban"—Annotation: Li Ciming says "faction ban" should read "ban on factions" [黨禁].
translations.idiomatic: *禁黨 → 黨禁*.

sentences.zh: 二一七三頁二行而讓懷協忿怨汲本、殿本「協」作「挾」。

translations.literal: Page 2173 line 2 "and Rang harbored *xie* resentment"—Ji and Palace editions write *xie* [挟] for *xie* [協].
translations.idiomatic: The Ji and Palace manuscripts substitute the graph commonly read as "embrace" or "coerce" for the graph usually read as "harmony," a frequent interchange in these editions.

sentences.zh: 按：協挾古字通，黨錮傳「懷經協術」，黃瓊傳「黃門協邪」，皆借「協」為「挾」也。

translations.literal: Annotation: *xie* and *xie* are ancient variants; *Danggu* "cherished classics *xie* craft"; Huang Qiong "Yellow Gates *xie* evil" all borrow *xie* for *xie*.
translations.idiomatic: 協/挾 graphic loans in parallel texts.

sentences.zh: 二一七三頁三行明年遂傳下獄按：校補引柳從辰說，謂「明年」二字衍，蓋黃巾起事及允之討擊黃巾別帥，發張讓之奸，皆中平元年二三月事，下獄會赦，還復剌史，旬日閒復以它罪被補，仍不出元年三月也。

translations.literal: Page 2173 line 3 "the next year was sent down to prison"—Annotation: Liu Congchen argues "next year" is redundant: Yellow Turban rising and Wang Yun’s campaign against a Turban leader exposing Zhang Rang—all first month of Zhongping 1; prison and amnesty still within that month.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Congchen concludes that the words meaning "the following year" are an interpolation because every episode referenced belongs to the second or third month of the first Zhongping year.

sentences.zh: 二一七三頁九行太尉袁隗司徒楊賜通鑒考異謂隗、賜時皆不為此官，恐誤。

translations.literal: Page 2173 line 9 "Grand Commandant Yuan Wei, Minister of Education Yang Ci"—*Zizhi tongjian kaoyi* doubts Wei and Ci held those offices—likely error.
translations.idiomatic: *通鉴考异* questions titles.

sentences.zh: 按：

translations.literal: Annotation:
translations.idiomatic: Comment:

sentences.zh: 通鑒系此事於中平元年冬十二月，故考異云然。

translations.literal: *Zizhi tongjian* dates this to Zhongping 1 winter twelfth month—hence *kaoyi*.
translations.idiomatic: Because Sima Guang placed the episode in the twelfth month of Zhongping 1, his "Scrutiny of divergences" commentary raises questions about the offices named.

sentences.zh: 柳從辰謂隗、賜之與何進共上疏請，乃在元年二三月閒，其時袁隗為司徒，楊賜為太尉，不過官名互誤耳。

translations.literal: Liu Congchen says Wei and Ci’s joint memorial with He Jin was in first–second month of year 1; then Yuan Wei was Minister of Education and Yang Ci Grand Commandant—only the titles were swapped in the text.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Congchen replies that the memorial belongs to the early months of Zhongping 1 and that Yuan Wei then held the ministry while Yang Ci commanded the army—the narrative simply transposes the two titles.

sentences.zh: 二一七六頁八行今若一旦解兵*

translations.literal: Page 2176 line 8 "if now in one morning you disarm them*"
translations.idiomatic: if you disarm—

sentences.zh: (關東)

translations.literal: (east of the pass)
translations.idiomatic: (Guandong)

sentences.zh: *刊誤謂案文多「關東」二字。

translations.literal: *"*—*Kanwu*: the text improperly duplicates "east of the pass."
translations.idiomatic: *Kanwu*: redundant 關東.

sentences.zh: 今據刪。

translations.literal: Now deleted accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Deleted.

sentences.zh: 按：集解引王補說，謂通鑒作「解兵開關」。

translations.literal: Annotation: Jijie cites Wang Bu: *Zizhi tongjian* reads "disarm and open the passes."
translations.idiomatic: Wang Bu notes that Sima Guang’s text instead speaks of disarming the troops and opening the barrier passes.

sentences.zh: 二一七六頁一一行丁彥思蔡伯喈但以董公親厚並尚從坐按：集解引洪亮吉說，謂丁彥思不知何人，陳、范二史於卓傳俱不載，裴松之注極詳，亦不及此。

translations.literal: Page 2176 line 11 "Ding Yansi, Cai Bosai, only because they were intimate with Lord Dong, still suffered collective punishment"—Jijie cites Hong Liangji: Ding Yansi is unknown; neither Chen nor Fan histories nor Pei’s commentary records him.
translations.idiomatic: Hong Liangji observes that Ding Yansi cannot be identified because neither standard history of the period nor Pei Songzhi’s exhaustive notes mention such a person alongside Cai Yong.

sentences.zh: 又引王補說，謂通鑒無「丁彥思」三字。

translations.literal: Also citing Wang Bu: *Zizhi tongjian* lacks the three characters "Ding Yansi."
translations.idiomatic: Wang Bu adds that Sima Guang’s digest omits Ding Yansi’s name altogether.

sentences.zh: 二一七八頁三行封其孫黑為安樂亭侯按：校補引柳從辰說，謂袁紀「黑」作「異」。

translations.literal: Page 2178 line 3 "enfeoffed his grandson Hei as marquis of Anle hamlet"—Annotation: Liu Congchen cites *Yuan ji* writing "Hei" as "Yi."
translations.idiomatic: *袁纪* 黑 vs 異.
